issue_title: Client gets stuck in disconnected state
issue_body:
Lately with [pmxbot](/yougov/pmxbot), we're observing a condition where intermittently (about once a week) the client will lose its connection to the server, and once in that state, any attempts to transmit messages results in a ServerNotConnectedError exception.

The server should probably respond more robustly under this condition, either automatically reconnecting or by documenting a recommendation for how a client implementation should handle this condition.

issue_title: SIUnitx Formatter can't handle fractional powers
issue_body:
Hello,
I am using Pint 0.6, but I also set up a separate function to duplicate the siunitx formatter for myself, since it doesn't exist in 0.6. In working with it, I found that it is unable to print fractional powers (e.g. 0.5) because the power is expected to be an integer: https://github.com/hgrecco/pint/blob/5c409ea6695170b74f63ba39fbc64162c482730b/pint/formatting.py#L206

A simple fix is to test if the power is an integer:
    
    return r'\tothe{{{:d}}}'.format(int(power)) if power.is_integer() else r'\tothe{{{:.1f}}}'.format(power)

Of course, knowing the precision required for the float is the next problem.

issue_title: bug: annotation marks appearing only after reload
issue_body: The annotation marks above the words indicating the spans of each annotation is shown only after reloading the page (not immediately after adding the annotation).

In one of my applications, I use the following:

```python
pop_1 = A_1 * exp(-(x - x0_1)**2/(2 * sig_1**2))
pop_2 = A_2 * exp(-(x - x0_2)**2/(2 * sig_2**2))
model = pop_1 + pop_2

fit = Fit(model, xdata, ydata)
fit_result = fit.execute()
```
After fitting, the model is easily evaluated using 
```python
y = model(x=xdata, **fit_result.params)
```
But what if you only want to evaluate pop_1? The following currently gives an error:
```python
y_1 = pop_1(x=xdata, **fit_result.params)
```
Should this be supported, or do we want another shorthand for doing this? If you have a good idea, please propose your suggestion as a reply to this issue.

issue_title: Setting a timeout on requests to Zendesk's API
issue_body:
Hey! We are noticing that some requests to Zendesk's API will hang indefinitely. Would you be able to modify your zendesk client to allow for the setting of a timeout? I am not sure at what level the timeout should be applied, but here are a couple of ideas:
- Global timeout on the Zenpy object, for example:
```python
zenpy = Zenpy(**creds, timeout=<int>)
```
- timeout keyword argument on the method, for example:
```python
zenpy.users.create_or_update(zenpy_user, timeout=<int>)
```

On an Ubuntu 16.04 computer with bluez 5.43, GATTToolBackend returns incorrect device names when scanning:

Debug output when adding the following line after line 300 in backends/gatttools/gatttools.py: `print(line)`
```
LE Scan ...
7F:6C:9D:31:F1:DC (unknown)
INFO:pygatt.backends.gatttool.gatttool:Discovered 7F:6C:9D:31:F1:DC (None)
A0:E6:F8:75:50:84 BF PPG Project
INFO:pygatt.backends.gatttool.gatttool:Discovered A0:E6:F8:75:50:84 (BF)

```
In this example **BF PPG Project** is incorrectly identified as **BF**. This is a result of name being by matched by the regular expression `\(?[\w]+\)?`.

However, the Bluetooth spec does not impose the name to be one word, nor to be only alpha-numeric characters:

Section 12.1:
> The Device Name characteristic shall contain the name of the device as an UTF-8 string as defined in Section 3.2.2. [...] The Device Name characteristic value shall be 0 to 248 octets in length. A device shall have only one instance of the Device Name characteristic.

 `\(?[\w]+\)?` should be changed to  `\(?.+\)?`

Every now and then I will get a message printed out when using the Pygatt library:

    No handlers culd be found for logger "pygatt.backends.gatttool.gatttool"

I haven't had a chance to check out the source for this myself, so I'm not sure if it's actually Pygatt's fault or not. But, if there's anything that can be done about it, that'd be great!

issue_title: Documents with a large number of Pages nodes cause RecursionError
issue_body:
I'm not completely familiar with the PDF spec, so I apologize if my terminology is incorrect. 

If a PDF has a large number of /Type /Pages nodes, instantiating PdfReader causes a RecursionError: maximum recursion depth exceeded while calling a Python object in readpages().readnode()
 
The break point seems to be around 1954 nodes in the document, though that number varies depending on how nested the code is that creates the PdfReader object. This was found while attempting to split the pages from a document created with an application that uses WebSupergoo's ABCpdf.NET library (https://www.websupergoo.com/products.htm#abcpdf).  The pdfrw module is used as part of automation testing of this product. The initial file that triggered it was a real-world file, not a test file, so this will be encountered in the wild.
 
A solution might be to modify readpages().readnode() to not use recursion. One approach would be to use lists that track the Page objects found as well as the Pages/Catalogs objects that need to be processed. This solution is included in the attached file.
 
[pdfrw.zip](https://github.com/pmaupin/pdfrw/files/974324/pdfrw.zip) contains the following files:
- reecusion_test.py - The code from my automation that triggers this. Modify the source_file and output_directory variables as necessary.
- pdf_generator.py - A module that will let you generate simple PDF files with as many pages as you need. The destination_directory will need to be changed. It depends on reportlab.
- merger.cs - C# code that uses the ABCpdf.NET library to merge the files created by pdf_generator.py
- 977.pdf - This file reproduces the problem. It consists of 977 merged single page documents
- 976.pdf - This file does not reproduce the problem. It consists of 976 merged single page documents
- 97600.pdf - This file contains 97,600 merged 100 page documents and does not reproduce the error. It may be useful for performance comparison
- pdfreader.py - Contains a proposed fix.
 
The changes to pdfreader.py are:
- The __init__ takes a new parameter, use_readpages_new. If True, it uses the proposed fix. If False, it uses the original code (allowing for performance comparisons).
- A new method: use_readpages_new that contains a non-recursive readnode() function.
 
Testing performed on pdfreader.py:
- Ran all three PDFs through pdf_generator.py and compared performance of both methods. Memory usage and overall time was roughly identical. Due to time restraints, I didn't extract each page and only timed how long it took to parse the pages.
- Ran 976.pdf through pdf_generator.py with both methods and did a binary compare of the output files to ensure the extracted pages were correct. All files matched exactly, indicating that the pages not only were extracted properly, but that the order was correct.

issue_title: [PATCH] RFC1213-MIB::atTable indices are not encoded/resolved correctly
issue_body:
Version: Branch master at commit 0f32c00d89eae2f86b2282ffc50805e059be5492
 
pysnmp/smi/mibs/RFC1213-MIB.py defines the `atNetAddress` index of `atTable` erroneously as `IpAddress`, while the correct type is `NetworkAddress`.  Since the two types have different OID encoding (`NetworkAddress` is equivalent to the modern `InetAddressType`-`InetAddress` combo and has an extra octet at the beginning which specifies the address type), PySNMP fails to encode or decode `atNetAddress` index.

To reproduce:

```
import pysnmp.debug
from pysnmp.smi.builder import MibBuilder
from pysnmp.smi.view import MibViewController
from pysnmp.smi.rfc1902 import ObjectIdentity
from pysnmp.proto.rfc1155 import IpAddress
from pysnmp.proto.rfc1902 import ObjectName
import user

builder = MibBuilder()
builder.loadModules('RFC1213-MIB')
view = MibViewController(builder)

pysnmp.debug.setLogger(pysnmp.debug.Debug('all'))

ident = ObjectIdentity('RFC1213-MIB', 'atIfIndex', 7, IpAddress('143.248.1.177'))
ident.resolveWithMib(view)
print(ident.getOid())

ident2 = ObjectIdentity(ObjectName('1.3.6.1.2.1.3.1.1.1.7.1.143.248.1.177'))
ident2.resolveWithMib(view)
print(ident2.getMibSymbol())
```

Results in:

```

2017-10-06 10:37:45,796 pysnmp: getNodeNameByOid: resolved :(1, 3, 6, 1, 2, 1, 3, 1, 1) -> ('iso', 'org', 'dod', 'internet', 'mgmt', 'mib-2', 'at', 'atTable', 'atEntry').()
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/opt/local/Library/Frameworks/Python.framework/Versions/3.6/lib/python3.6/runpy.py", line 193, in _run_module_as_main

  File "/Users/eugene.kim/venvs/playground36/lib/python3.6/site-packages/pysnmp-4.4.1-py3.6.egg/pysnmp/smi/rfc1902.py", line 450, in resolveWithMib
  File "<string>", line 1223, in getIndicesFromInstId
pysnmp.smi.error.SmiError: Excessive instance identifier sub-OIDs left at MibTableRow((1, 3, 6, 1, 2, 1, 3, 1, 1), None): 177

Note:

- The OID-encoded `atNetAddress` `143.248.1.177` lacks the `NetworkAddress` type selector value `1` at the beginning;
- The buggy code could not resolve a correctly encoded index (`ident2`) because it did not expect the `NetworkAddress` type selector value before the IP address literal.  What happened instead is: 1) the buggy code erroneously decoded the address as `1.143.248.1` (the type selector value plus the first 3 sub-identifiers of the IP address), then 2) raised an exception upon seeing 177, which the last octet of the IP address.

issue_title: python-keyring-lib should have some form of continuous integration tests
issue_body:
python-keyring-lib has a nice suite of tests and supports various platforms and environments.
[Obvious](https://bitbucket.org/kang/python-keyring-lib/issue/84/python-3-incompatibility-in-google-backend) [things](https://bitbucket.org/kang/python-keyring-lib/issue/56/cannot-run-the-test-suite-under-python-3) are breaking sometimes, and it would be nice if we can get an early feedback on what's going on.

Github has Travis-CI which is really cool and easy to set up, but it's not available for Bitbucket sadly.
However, considering the extended support of various OS and environments, something like Buildbot running Buildslave on different OS would be great (especially for devs who don't have access to all the OS supported).

Anyway, even without looking for something too much complicated, *something* which could run common tests after each commit and report somewhere here would already be interesting.

----------------------------------------
- Bitbucket: https://bitbucket.org/kang/python-keyring-lib/issue/86
- Originally reported by: Jonathan Ballet
- Originally created at: 2013-02-07T09:13:23.404

Thus far, `meshio.medit_io` only reads and writes text MEDIT mesh whereas according to Frey ([2001](https://hal.inria.fr/inria-00069921), §7.2.1):
> This format is composed of a single (binary or text) data file.

This would be of much more interest in the context of nschloe/pygmsh#212 if Gmsh wrote binary MEDIT mesh but it appears to ignore its `-bin` flag for `-format mesh`.  I'm not sure how common the binary form is in the wild.

Implement more mesh formats, e.g.,

 * Dolfin's XML,
 * METIS (.gra)
 * Scotch (.grf),
 * diffpack (.grid),
 * Abaqus (.inp)

issue_title: Repeating item's @f key and values mistakenly included when editing "1: this instance"
issue_body:
When an attempt is made to edit a repeating item and the option "**1: this instance**" is chosen, the text loaded into the editor *includes* the repeating item's ***@f*** key and values.  

In this circumstance, the ***@f*** key and its associated values should be *excluded*, just as they are when the option "**2: this and all subsequent instances**" is chosen.

issue_title: How can I make requests in response to updates?
issue_body:
Hello,
as per telegram faq , you can [make request in response to updates](https://core.telegram.org/bots/faq#how-can-i-make-requests-in-response-to-updates), removing the need for another request. while this may not be optimal for sending messages or other methods due to inability to know that such a request was successful or get its result, but it can help speed up inline queries.
the way i can see it we can implant it in one of these ways:

- create a new function for each method that instead of making the request returns the json to be sent as response.
- add a parameter to Bot class `__init__` to chose its behavior between making a post request or returning json
- add a parameter to each function method to define behavior , something like `return_response=False`

i prefer the last one and i can make a pull request for it if you think it is a good addition to telepot,
i'm open to other ideas on how to add this feature though.

issue_title: Fall back to multiple message deletes in purge.
issue_body:
A note to myself.

When a message's snowflake is older than 2 weeks old it should change the deletion strategy to single message deletes.

Since the change employs different deletion strategies, this would also be a good opportunity to make it so user bots can use `purge_from` as well as a high level layer. 

A drawback to this approach is that the internal deletion strategies end up propagating different exceptions in different cases when the strategy switches. e.g. you don't need MANAGE_MESSAGES as a bot to delete your own messages when done one by one but for a real bot the bulk delete endpoint does require it.

issue_title: ensure_coordinator_ready loops indefinitely if coordinator not ready
issue_body:
Hello,

I'm neither experienced with kafka in general nor with kafka-python in particular, so bear with me if there is already an obvious solution to that problem.

We are using kafka in a kubernetes environment, so far only for staging experiments. We are using the robot framework for integration testing. We have some tests that check if logs have been successfully written to kafka, for that we are using kafka-python and a consumer to consume the corresponding topics. Normally this works as expected, but apparently there are still some bugs in our kubernetes setup, that is to say something can go wrong with kafka and/or zookeeper without liveness probes indicating any problem (but actually that's beside the point, we need to fix that as well of course). In that case the robot tests run indefinitely without any indication of a problem (might be because we do not see kafka-python logs because of the robot framework in between). After some debugging it turns out that the method ensure_coordinator_ready in coordinator/base.py goes into an endless loop if there is no coordinator available. As far as I can tell there is no timeout for this method, even if already the initial creation failed.

There were a few issues already with similar problems, but I haven't seen any of those specifically about this method. Is there already a solution to that problem, or does it make sense to add a timeout to this method as well (as there are already quite a few timeouts for different parts of the library).

Kind regards,
Michael

        consumer = KafkaConsumer('python', value_deserializer=lambda m: json.loads(m.decode('utf8')), **self.kafka)

I noticed the `<x509v3BasicConstraints>` is a plain string. The BasicConstraints extensions can be defined in short form `CA:FALSE pathLen:0` or so called [long form](https://www.openssl.org/docs/apps/x509v3_config.html). I think it would be useful to fully parse the basic constraints into key:value pairs.

issue_title: `lstat()` dominates in the case of small coverage samples
issue_body:
The hypothesis library recently added coverage-led fuzzing, in which it needs to a very short test many times, while examining the coverage between each trial. This (currently) involves many calls to `coverage.Collector.save_data`, which in turn causes many calls to `realpath` (and thus `lstat`). [In the extreme case](https://gist.github.com/bukzor/3e80b1d387bf68cd36198e1402bde8df), `lstat()` ends up taking about 40% of the runtime.

Can you please help us design a remedy? Some alternatives that I can think of:

1.  add a cache to files.abs_file
2. replace [the call to abs_file](https://github.com/nedbat/coveragepy/blob/coverage-4.4.2/coverage/collector.py#L400) with a call to files.canonical_path, since it already has a cache
3. Delegate the filename-normalization responsibility from Collector to CoverageData, such that we can specialize CoverageData and fix this within our dependent library.

If I try to use smart open to seek/read parts of an s3 file, I get `NotImplementedError: seek other than offset=0 not implemented yet`.

Arbitrary seeking, especially when the seek was specified relative to the beginning of the file (`seek(..., whence=0)`, should be possible through the `Range` HTTP header

```
>>> import boto
>>> s3 = boto.connect_s3()
>>> bucket = s3.lookup('bucket')
>>> key = bucket.lookup('key')
>>> parts = key.get_contents_as_string(headers={'Range' : 'bytes=12-24'})
```

`seek` could establish a pointer to the starting byte and subsequent `read`s would define the end.

Are there any technical limitation or design restrictions that would prevent this?

issue_title: No way to pass payload via action() method, but some HTTP routes require a body
issue_body:
ArangoDB version: 3.3.12
pyArango version: 1.3.1 (via pip)

See https://stackoverflow.com/questions/51362259/rename-collection-with-pyarango

To rename a collection, it is necessary to pass a JSON body `{"name":"newname"}` in a PUT request to `/_api/collection/<collectionHandle>/rename`. The `.action()` method only supports passing on URL path _params_ however.

The HTTP docs also describe that there would be a path parameter `?collection-name=newname` but it is not support anymore (I tried with curl but got errors back about name being empty as if no parameter for name or collection-name was passed).

It would be nice if the action method and possibly others too would support passing a body.

In a small experiment, I found that repeating literal terms was faster than moving them to a rule as the README suggests. I was testing a JSON grammar. Here is the JSON input:

```json
    {
        "bool": [
            true,
            false
        ],
        "number": {
            "float": -0.14e3,
            "int": 1
        },
        "other": {
            "string": "string",
            "unicode": "あ",
            "null": null
        }
    }
```

Here is the first implementation (with rules)

```
        Start    = Object / Array
        Object   = LBrace (Mapping (Comma Mapping)*)? RBrace
        Mapping  = DQString Colon Value
        Array    = LBracket (Value (Comma Value)*)? RBracket
        Value    = Object / Array / DQString
                 / TrueVal / FalseVal / NullVal / Float / Integer
        Spacing  = ~"\s*"
        Comma    = "," Spacing
        Colon    = ":" Spacing
        LBrace   = "{" Spacing
        RBrace   = "}" Spacing
        LBracket = "[" Spacing
        RBracket = "]" Spacing
        TrueVal  = "true" Spacing
        FalseVal = "false" Spacing
        NullVal  = "null" Spacing
        DQString = ~"\"[^\"\\\\]*(?:\\\\.[^\"\\\\]*)*\"" Spacing
        Float    = ~"[-+]?(\d+(\.\d*)?|\.\d+)([eE][-+]?\d+)?" Spacing
        Integer  = ~"[-+]?\d+" Spacing
```

With timeit over 10000 iterations, it took 3.84 seconds. Then I tried a grammar that expanded those literal terms:

```
        Start    = Object / Array
        Object   = "{" Spacing (Mapping (Spacing "," Spacing Mapping)*)? Spacing "}"
        Mapping  = DQString Spacing ":" Spacing Value
        Array    = "[" Spacing (Value (Spacing "," Spacing Value)*)? Spacing "]"
        Value    = Object / Array / DQString
                 / TrueVal / FalseVal / NullVal / Float / Integer
        Spacing  = ~"\s*"
        TrueVal  = "true"
        FalseVal = "false"
        NullVal  = "null"
        DQString = ~"\"[^\"\\\\]*(?:\\\\.[^\"\\\\]*)*\""
        Float    = ~"[-+]?(\d+(\.\d*)?|\.\d+)([eE][-+]?\d+)?"
        Integer  = ~"[-+]?\d+"
```
This version took just 2.98 seconds (22% speedup).

You might notice that many of those terms (e.g., braces, brackets, colons) don't repeat, so it's an unnecessary lookup. A version with the only repeated literal (the comma with surrounding spacing) written as a rule takes about 3.15 seconds (18% speedup). If incorporate all those Spacings into the literal strings (e.g. `~"{\s*", etc.), it takes 2.19 seconds (43% speedup), but that kinda feels like cheating.

It could be that my test string does not lend itself well to improvements by caching, since there's not a lot of ambiguity or backtracking required, but it seems at least that the README is somewhat inaccurate. Otherwise, though, it seems like gains from making terms into rules are lost by the rule lookup time.

The [Closest Meaning Logic Adapter](https://github.com/gunthercox/ChatterBot/blob/master/chatterbot/adapters/logic/closest_meaning.py) currently only checks the path similarity between the first synset for each word (`synset1[0].path_similarity(synset2[0])`). This heuristic might be more accurate if it selected the maximum (shortest path) pair of synsets for the two words.

Because this operation might greatly increase the amount of time required to process each result, it may be useful to look into the possibility of caching logical evaluations made by this adapter on the statement object when it is saved to the database. Then, the check would only need to be processed if the value of the overall synonymous meaning between two statements had not already been evaluated.

issue_title: appropriate settings and difference from ffmpeg
issue_body:
Hi, I am not reporting a bug; I just have a usage question about this script.  First of all, I am confused about what the value-add is over ffmpeg.  But maybe there is one because I have tried to use ffmpeg for volume normalization and the results were not very satisfactory.

My application is that I want to use librivox audiobooks as training data for a machine learning model.  These audiobooks are recorded under varying conditions with varying quality.

I tried using ffmpeg with the following option: `af 'dynaudnorm=c=1:r=1:p=1'`.  I found that this seemed to do a bad job in the following sense. Also, if you happen to know of similar scripts to remove background noise or other artifacts of amateur recordings, I would be glad to know about them.

issue_title: Session.sign will fill the memory and cause a SIGABRT when data argument is a very big integer
issue_body:
#### Your system information

* PyKCS11 version: 1.4.4
* Python version: 3.6.4

#### Please describe your issue in as much detail as possible:
When calling the `session.sign(key, data)` function if the `data` argument is a integer then a `ckbytelist` will be created using `ckbytelist(data)` [[1]](https://github.com/LudovicRousseau/PyKCS11/blob/f332518d4f61ba301e39e4ecc03d6ba5ca0f8410/PyKCS11/__init__.py#L1039), this will create a `ckbytelist` of size `data`[[2]](https://github.com/LudovicRousseau/PyKCS11/blob/f332518d4f61ba301e39e4ecc03d6ba5ca0f8410/PyKCS11/__init__.py#L77) so if `data` is a very big number the process will crash with 
```
terminate called after throwing an instance of 'std::bad_alloc'
  what():  std::bad_alloc

Process finished with exit code 134 (interrupted by signal 6: SIGABRT)
```
#### Steps for reproducing this issue:

I can reproduce the error with
```python
import PyKCS11
if __name__ == '__main__':
    bignum = 24401231876787445786723
    print(bignum.bit_length())
    pk = PyKCS11.PyKCS11Lib()
    pk.load("libpteidpkcs11.so")
    slots = pk.getSlotList()
    if len(slots) < 1:
        exit()
    session = pk.openSession(slots[0])
    key = session.findObjects(template=((PyKCS11.CKA_LABEL, "CITIZEN AUTHENTICATION KEY"),
                                             (PyKCS11.CKA_CLASS, PyKCS11.CKO_PRIVATE_KEY),
                                             (PyKCS11.CKA_KEY_TYPE, PyKCS11.CKK_RSA)))[0]
    mec = PyKCS11.Mechanism(PyKCS11.CKM_SHA1_RSA_PKCS, "")
    signed = session.sign(key, bignum, mec)
    print(signed)
```
 
To fix this maybe you can check if `data` is a integer in the sign function and convert it to a byte array or just throw an exception.

P.S. The buzzer and LED control don't affect the bug. If removed, the led is turned off for 2 seconds after the communication.
I am using Python27x64 on Windows10.

Here is my code:
`class PrintObserver(CardObserver):

				data, sw1, sw2 = card.connection.transmit(self.Auth)

issue_title: Propagate API exceptions in Django backend
issue_body:
With batch sending all API exceptions are hidden, because API responds with a list of results and 200 HTTP code. Only non 4xx and 5xx codes will cause an exception.

In case if there is one or more errors in batch response, then exception should be risen (with appropriate ``fail_silently`` logic).

Probably, multiple errors could be combined in a single one.

issue_title: Mismatched italics syntax causes template filter to fail sometimes
issue_body:
When there's mismatched italics syntax, the `.filter_templates()` method fails to properly parse the page sometimes.

Sample script:

```python
#! /usr/bin/env python

import mwparserfromhell

def parse_text(case_text):
    parsed_page_text = mwparserfromhell.parse(case_text)
    print(len(parsed_page_text.filter_templates()))
    for template in parsed_page_text.filter_templates():
        print(template.name.strip())

case_text = """\
{{Infobox SCOTUS case
  |FullName=''[[et vir]]'
}}

'''''Hello there'''''
"""

parse_text(case_text)

case_text = """\
{{Infobox SCOTUS case
  |FullName=''[[et vir]]''
}}

'''''Hello there'''''
"""

parse_text(case_text)

case_text = """\
{{Infobox SCOTUS case
  |FullName=''[[et vir]]'
}}
"""

parse_text(case_text)
```

Of note: `  |FullName=''[[et vir]]'` is mismatched in the first case and the third case.

Current buggy output:

```
0
1
Infobox SCOTUS case
1
Infobox SCOTUS case
```

Expected output:

```
1
Infobox SCOTUS case
1
Infobox SCOTUS case
1
Infobox SCOTUS case
```

I think I may be seeing a performance issue. I'm using legacy.text() and draw.point() to light text (the time) plus a few pixels on 4 cascaded matrix displays. When doing this in a simple test script it works great. However, when moving this into my larger script I'm seeing flashing of the pixels I'm lighting individually (legacy.text is solid). Code is below. I can upload a video of the behavior I'm seeing if you'd like.

Thanks for your work here! Other than this flashing issue I've had a really easy time getting up a running. Great examples and documentation.

#
#### Type of Raspberry Pi 
RPi3

#### Linux Kernel version
Linux piclock 4.4.34-v7+ #930 SMP Wed Nov 23 15:20:41 GMT 2016 armv7l GNU/Linux

#### Expected behaviour
Pixels change to desired state and do not flash in transition or while lit.

#### Actual behaviour
Seeing pixels flashing either while in transition or while lit.

#
Here's the code that works in this test script, but causes flashing behavior in my larger script:
```
#!/usr/bin/env python

from luma.core import legacy
from luma.led_matrix.device import max7219
from luma.core.serial import spi, noop
from luma.core.render import canvas

import datetime, time



# create matrix device
serial = spi(port=0, device=0, gpio=noop())
device = max7219(serial, cascaded=4, block_orientation="vertical")

clock_display = (datetime.datetime.now().strftime('%I:%M'))

show_dots = True
counter = 1

while True:
    if show_dots == True:
        with canvas(device) as draw:
            legacy.text(draw, (0, 0), clock_display, fill="white", font=legacy.font.proportional(legacy.font.SINCLAIR_FONT))
            draw.point([(31, 7), (30, 7), (31, 6)], fill="white")
    elif show_dots == False:
        with canvas(device) as draw:
            legacy.text(draw, (0, 0), clock_display, fill="white", font=legacy.font.proportional(legacy.font.SINCLAIR_FONT))

    time.sleep(.5)
    counter += 1
    
    if counter == 8:
        show_dots = not show_dots
        counter = 1
```

This is probably interpreted too late, aka `setup.py` can't handle such a statement:
https://github.com/rm-hull/luma.led_matrix/blob/c93ee585b9131503cdd709ef83140e90882cd905/setup.py#L29

issue_title: DATE_INPUT_FORMATS is now a list instead of a tuple (Django 1.9)
issue_body:

`CSV_DATE_INPUT_FORMATS = DATE_INPUT_FORMATS + ('%d-%m-%Y', '%Y-%m-%d')`

In Django 1.9, DATE_INPUT_FORMATS is a list, so this operation is invalid.

issue_title: How to create subnamespace for swagger documentation?
issue_body:
The problem is that within a single namespace there might be many api endpoints making the list too long to read. Move part of them into another namespace is OK. But in swagger documentation it becomes a peer rather then a folded subnamespace. I want to preserve the hierarchical order for example:

in original swagger documentation:
person:
    -------[get]person/pid
    -------[get]-person/device
    -------[post]person/device
    --------...... many other endpoints 

I wish I could make it clearer and preserve the hierarchical structure like this:

person:
    -------[get]person/pid
    -------device:
             -------[post]person/device
             -------[get]person/device
     --------...... many other endpoints

According to documentation:

> **# Response marshalling**
> You can define a dict or OrderedDict of fields whose keys are names of attributes or keys on the object to render, and whose values are a class that will format & return the value for that field. 

API Model constructor:

```
class Model(dict, MutableMapping):

    def __init__(self, name, *args, **kwargs):
        ...
        super(Model, self).__init__(*args, **kwargs)`
        ...
```

As you can see Model class extends from ordinary dict and uses its constructor.
That breaks custom fields order of any dict you will try to set in.

issue_title: Setting/Getting boolean values yields a ValueError
issue_body:
In Python Memcached 1.56 (w/ six-1.9.0 & Python 2.7.10)

doing

cache.set('foo', False)
cache.get('foo')

generates a ValueError: invalid literal for int() with base 10: 'False'

line 1235 memcache.py

When the method _val_to_store_info is called the path code that it is followed with a boolean is

       elif isinstance(val, int):
            flags |= Client._FLAG_INTEGER
            val = str(val)
            if six.PY3:
                val = val.encode('ascii')
            # force no attempt to compress this silly string.
            min_compress_len = 0

Since the flags bits have been set to an integer, the boolean value is changed to a string ('False'). So in the method _recv_value the code tries to change that string to an int which fails .

In Python Memcached 1.54

the original code in _val_to_store_info is

elif isinstance(val, int):
            flags |= Client._FLAG_INTEGER
            val = "%d" % val
            # force no attempt to compress this silly string.
            min_compress_len = 0

the issue also affect when checking for long values

issue_title: Bug when obtaining distinct years from date
issue_body:
It appears that the logic on this line causes problems with years that are only two digits. I know that this is a pretty uncommon case, however it is something to consider.

The logic on this line given a date in SQL Server as '0017-01-01' will cause that logic to error. Python's datetime library accepts two digit years, for example:

`
from datetime import datetime
datetime(17, 1, 1)
`

https://github.com/michiya/django-pyodbc-azure/blob/4df37f3ec40abaf1aca608e1922a93073db8f144/sql_server/pyodbc/operations.py#L192

The problem seems to be when you obtain the Year in the SQL it provides a 2 digit value. When casting back into a datetime it creates an invalid format of "17-01-01".

The user in question has been granted all permissions in the admin section.

Key code listed below - any help much appreciated

**test_app/models.py**

    class Article(models.Model):
        created_by = models.ForeignKey(User)
        created = models.DateField(auto_now_add=True)

    from permission import add_permission_logic

issue_title: Don't count the coverage for the entire project if your testing just one app
issue_body:
So let's say I run something like ``coverage run manage.py test && coverage html && open htmlcov/index.html`` normally to test my entire project and I have ``source = .`` set in my ``.coveragerc`` file. Is there way to make the source or the ``coverage html --include`` automatically adjust if I'm just running tests for one particular Django app that's within my project (i.e. ``coverage run manage.py test apps.foo``).

Right now I'm just manually typing out a command that's getting a little unwieldy and this command is actually sorter now because I'm now using a ``.coveragerc`` file to omit things.

```
coverage run --source="apps/foo" manage.py test apps.foo && coverage html && open htmlcov/index.html
```

I tried making a Makefile rule like ``test_coverage`` that would normally just include all the source in the html report, but then only include the apps source code if I'm testing just one django app within my project, but I gave up because my simple ``ifdef`` statement wasn't working. 

Anyway, is there anyway to include this logic for only showing the source in the html report if the person ran tests for just one app?

Would it be an idea to automatically set `detect_acronyms` to `True` if any non-empty list of acronyms is passed? Now, a user must always specify both the acronyms and the boolean, while this is unnecessary. It would only be required to pass both arguments if a user passes a non-empty list of acronyms, but does not want to use them, so: if `detect_acronyms=False` is passed explicitly, only then any acronyms are ignored; otherwise, any acronyms are detected automatically (none by default).

It looks like the URLs created from sass + less are being created as something like, `.../Roboto/Roboto-Light.woff2`, instead of `.../roboto/Roboto-Light.woff2`. On a case-sensitive filesystem (Linux, in my case), this means that the files can't be found.

Any thoughts on the best way to address this? A few options I thought of:

1. Use sass's `to-lower-case` function, but less lacks a similar function.
1. Add an extra, properly-cased arg to the mixin, but that's just a lot of repeated crap.

issue_title: Fail to handle exception log records
issue_body:
Hello,
I encounter some troubles to send exception log records with your handler, it raises an exception in the flush method and prevents all the buffer to be sent. 
After some researches, I found the origin of the problem in the elasticsearch module that you use. Inside the module, there is a module named "serializer.py" containing a "JSONSerializer" class that does not support the exception type contained in the record.exc_info.

I see two possibilites to resolve the problem, the first is to create your own JSONSerializer which can support the exception type. You can easily pass it in the ElasticSearch constructor like this:
`self._client = Elasticsearch(hosts=self.hosts, serializer=customJSONSerializer(), ...) `
      
Indeed, the elasticsearch module is able to take custom serializer class.
The second possibility is to test if the record contains an exception type in your emit method, like this:
`if record.exc_info: 
   record.exc_info = None`

Please, just confirm this bug, I will take care of the fix in a pull request. 
Thank you in advance

issue_title: Daphne doesn't drop connections when channel layer goes down
issue_body:
## Bug description
Hi all,
As it is said in ASGI specification (https://channels.readthedocs.io/en/1.1.8/asgi.html):

**That said, if a channel server goes down momentarily and loses all data, persistent socket connections will continue to transfer incoming data and send out new generated data, but will have lost all of their group memberships and in-flight messages.
In order to avoid a nasty set of bugs caused by these half-deleted sockets, protocol servers should quit and hard restart if they detect that the channel layer has gone down or lost data; shedding all existing connections and letting clients reconnect will immediately resolve the problem.**

and I expect that Daphne server drops all connections when detects that channel layer is down until it goes up thus preventing new messages to be sent.

Instead of that I see that Daphne server keeps open all connections which are unusable. I don't receive anything via them even after channel layer is up and running (including `channels` workers)

Using `asgi_redis` packages as channel layer shows that the `channels` workers are got restarted after receiving `redis.exceptions.ConnectionError`:

I believe the problem is in the line `daphne/server.py:141`, where it is using `try ... except Exception` which covers all possible exceptions which is also covers the case when it should drop all open connections instead of waiting 5 seconds.
```

I have some problems,Can anyone help me please?
I want to use some specific API function in https://github.com/hpi-xnor/BMXNet

$ git clone --recursive https://github.com/hpi-xnor/mxnet.git # remember to include the --recursive
$ mkdir build/Release && cd build/Release
$ cmake ../../
$ make -j8
$ export LD_LIBRARY_PATH=/build/Release
$ export PYTHONPATH=/python

Any help would be appericated! Thanks a lot!

Best Regards,
PengWei

the training speed of BN-Inception is 10,666 seconds per epoch for one TitanX
see https://github.com/dmlc/mxnet/tree/master/example/image-classification
while in Torch, the training speed is 7686 seconds per epoch (192 ms per 32 images)
see http://torch.ch/blog/2016/02/04/resnets.html
the difference is not neglectable, do you know the reasons?

issue_title: Check failed in C prediction API
issue_body:
Before starting i want to thank the contributors of this amazing lib. I love the speed and effectiveness.

I come across a problem that seems very weird to me.
I downloaded and tested image_classifiction.cc on both Mac and Linux and i changed the input by giving a matrix instead of a name and get an error.

_Check failed: eshape[0] == 0 || eshape == dshape Assignment: Shape of Tensors are not consistent with target_

Unfortunately i don't get what esahpe and dshape are supposed to be.
Could you give me a pointer or something about it ?

**More infos** : I compiled MXNet under Mac with the one file trick and the .so lib for prediction only. Could it be linked ?

issue_title: Cannot reproduce a model using the model parameters.
issue_body:
## Environment info
Operating System:
CentOS 6.6

Compiler:
gcc-4.8

Package used (Python/R/Scala/Julia):
Python

MXNet version:

Or if installed from source:
from source

MXNet commit hash (`git rev-parse HEAD`):

If you are using python package, please provide

Python version and distribution:
Python 2.7.12 |Anaconda custom (64-bit)

## Error Message:
Cannot reproduce a model using the model parameters.

## Minimum reproducible example
I'm trying to reproduce the feed-forward step of an autoencoder by myself using python. I first train the model and save the params in files. Then I implement the feed-forward step and load the params. Then I feed data into it. If correctly implemented, the feed-forward step should yield the same output as that yielded by the original model in mxnet. However, the result I get is different.
I don't understand what each argument means, as show in the example [here](https://github.com/dmlc/mxnet/tree/master/example/autoencoder))

```
args = {'encoder_%d_weight'%istack: mx.nd.empty((num_hidden, num_input,global_kx,global_ky), self.xpu),
                'encoder_%d_bias'%istack: mx.nd.empty((num_hidden,), self.xpu),
                'decoder_%d_weight'%istack: mx.nd.empty((num_hidden, num_input,global_kx,global_ky), self.xpu),
                'decoder_%d_bias'%istack: mx.nd.empty((num_hidden,), self.xpu),}
args_grad = {'encoder_%d_weight'%istack: mx.nd.empty((num_hidden, num_input,global_kx,global_ky), self.xpu),
                     'encoder_%d_bias'%istack: mx.nd.empty((num_hidden,), self.xpu),
                     'decoder_%d_weight'%istack: mx.nd.empty((num_hidden, num_input,global_kx,global_ky), self.xpu),
                     'decoder_%d_bias'%istack: mx.nd.empty((num_hidden,), self.xpu),}
args_mult = {'encoder_%d_weight'%istack: 1.0,
                     'encoder_%d_bias'%istack: 2.0,
                     'decoder_%d_weight'%istack: 1.0,
                     'decoder_%d_bias'%istack: 2.0,}
auxs = {}
if encoder_act == 'sigmoid' and sparseness_penalty:
    auxs['sparse_encoder_%d_moving_avg' % istack] = mx.nd.ones((num_hidden), self.xpu) * 0.5
if decoder_act == 'sigmoid' and sparseness_penalty:
    auxs['sparse_decoder_%d_moving_avg' % istack] = mx.nd.ones((num_hidden), self.xpu) * 0.5
init = mx.initializer.Uniform(0.07)
for k,v in args.items():
    init(k,v)
```

I don't understand what each term means, i.e. `args`, `args_grad`, `args_mult`, `auxs`. I only know that the `args` is used to multiply the data items. I only save the `args` in files and load it into my own implementation. I did not load other params. I think this would be the reason why my implementation is different from mxnet. But what these params mean? Could someone tell me?
Thank you all for helping me!!!

issue_title: SQLAlchemy Table doesn't support storing as Kudu
issue_body: The `CREATE TABLE ... STORE AS KUDU` construct needed to create a Kudu internal table from Impala can be explicitly executed from Impyla, but it would be convenient if it could be added as a dialect-specific keyword argument in the SQLAlchemy connector.

should be: def pusher(url, count, size, poll, copy):
Not: def pusher(url, count, size, copy, poll):
Because the arguments are supplied by position from the main function.

Even though comments aren't meant to contain secrets, it's common for engineers to "comment out" secrets in YAML files, forgetting that SOPS does not encrypt them. We want to avoid accidentally having unencrypted secrets in SOPS files, so encrypting comments with AES just like other values would prevent this.

A few concerns I can think of right now:
- How do comments get added to the MAC?
- How do we keep backward compatibility?

When attempting to use celery-beat with the current master Django branch, I get the following error messages:
```
TypeError: __init__() missing 1 required positional argument: 'on_delete'
```
```
django_celery_beat/models.py:176: RemovedInDjango20Warning: on_delete will be a required arg for ForeignKey in Django 2.0. Set it to models.CASCADE on models and in existing migrations if you want to maintain the current default behavior. See https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/1.11/ref/models/fields/#django.db.models.ForeignKey.on_delete
  null=True, blank=True, verbose_name=_('interval'),
```
This is because of the deprecation mentioned here: https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/1.11/ref/models/fields/#django.db.models.ForeignKey.on_delete
```
Deprecated since version 1.9:
on_delete will become a required argument in Django 2.0. In older versions it defaults to CASCADE.
```

This should be safe to set explicitly to `CASCADE` on all `ForeignKey`s, as this will maintain legacy behavior, and `on_delete` has been an available option since before 1.8, which is the oldest version currently supported by this package.

issue_title: Power-off-period cronjob runs right after the first power-on-period cronjob ended instead of runs right after celerybeat starts
issue_body:
[anacron](https://serverfault.com/questions/52335/job-scheduling-using-crontab-what-will-happen-when-computer-is-shutdown-during)  quotes:  
`When your computer is shut down (or the cron daemon is otherwise not running), cron jobs will not be started. If you have jobs that you would like to run after the fact during those times when the computer is shut down, use anacron.`
I need to uncover today's product at 3 o'clock midnight in production. Celerybeat run well like anacron before 2016-12-04, but not after, on my computer. Here's the detail:
  
I use supervisor for the daemonize thing. Celery: v 4.0.0 django_celery_beat: v 1.0.1  
Cronjob 3'disclose set as '0 3 * * *'. 
Day 12-01 worked as expected, as soon as computer startup and supervisor run, task 3'disclose scheduled like anacron:  
```
[12-01 09:53:19,316] beat: Starting... (startup)
[12-01 09:53:19,507] Scheduler: Sending due task 3'disclose (homepage.tasks.disclose_today)
[12-02 01:23:30,357] poweroff
```
`12-02 01:23:30 ~ 12-02 10:40:24` computer was powered off.
Day 12-02 worked as expected.
```
[12-02 10:40:24,685] beat: Starting... (startup)
[12-02 10:40:24,867] Scheduler: Sending due task 3'disclose (homepage.tasks.disclose_today)
[12-03 00:39:08,070] poweroff
```
`12-03 00:39:08 ~ 12-03 11:00:29` computer was powered off.
Day 12-03 worked as expected.
```
[12-03 11:00:29,712] beat: Starting... (startup)
[12-03 11:00:29,892] Scheduler: Sending due task 3'disclose (homepage.tasks.disclose_today)
[12-04 02:02:48,290] poweroff
```
---------------------------- Here comes the wired thing ----------------------------
`12-04 02:02:48 ~ 12-04 09:46:25` computer was powered off. 
Day 12-04
```
[12-04 09:46:25,760] beat: Starting... (startup)
[12-04 12:00:00,022] Scheduler: Sending due task 12'greeting (homepage.tasks.greeting)
[12-04 12:01:00,005] Scheduler: Sending due task 12'greeting (homepage.tasks.greeting)
[12-04 12:...
[12-04 12:59:00,002] Scheduler: Sending due task 12'greeting (homepage.tasks.greeting)
[12-04 12:59:00,008] Scheduler: Sending due task 3'disclose (homepage.tasks.disclose_today)
[12-05 01:52:28,364] poweroff
Note the time 09:46:25 and 12:59:00, as the title describes.
Cronjob 12'greeting set as '* 12 * * *'. 
```
`12-05 01:52:28 ~ 12-05 10:45:32` computer was powered off.
Day 12-05
```
[12-05 10:45:32,857] beat: Starting... (startup)
[12-05 12:00:00,025] Scheduler: Sending due task 12'greeting (homepage.tasks.greeting)
[12-05 12:00:00,043] Scheduler: Sending due task 3'disclose (homepage.tasks.disclose_today)
[12-06 03:00:00,004] Scheduler: Sending due task 3'disclose (homepage.tasks.disclose_today)
[12-06 03:00:00,012] Scheduler: Sending due task celery.backend_cleanup (celery.backend_cleanup)
[12-06 04:00:00,004] Scheduler: Sending due task celery.backend_cleanup (celery.backend_cleanup)
[12-06 04:03:00,922] poweroff
Cronjob 12'greeting set as '0 12 * * *'. 
Note the behavior of backend_cleanup at 03:00:00
```
As far as I can remember, I changed timezone for celery app and add a cronjob 12'greeting for testing between day 12-03 and day 12-04.
Why? How to schedule 3'disclose as soon as celery beat starts like before 12-03?

issue_title: Schedule ad hoc job to be run immediately?
issue_body: Hi, I feel it would be a very useful feature for admins if they can just kick off a job on an ad-hoc basis. Would it make sense to add this?

Small correction.
https://github.com/miLibris/flask-rest-jsonapi/blob/master/flask_rest_jsonapi/exceptions.py#L4
It should be "Unknown error".

Have you considered reducing the amount of configuration boilerplate required? Are you open to pull requests which help implement defaults? Prompted by reading the [Logical data abstraction](https://web.archive.org/web/20171117060900/https://flask-rest-jsonapi.readthedocs.io/en/latest/logical_data_abstraction.html) - the comment says *we can see that Person has an attribute named “password”* but it's not actually easy to see that due to the duplication of most of the fields and related configuration.

Suggestions:
* automatically generate a marshmallow schema from the data layer and describe only differences in the schema layer when/where necessary. 
* automatically generate default endpoints

I understand that convention over configuration is a bit more of a Ruby/Rails thing, but sane defaults is a good thing.

In comparison, I've used Python eve before which has seems to have defaults (see https://web.archive.org/web/20170809171457/http://python-eve.org/config.html#global-configuration) and Flask-Restless seems to have sane defaults as well.

Yes, it seems no "six" module was installed from my side, but in "botocore/requirements.txt" file there was no mentioning about "six" dependency. 

In "utils.py" file, where an error occured, it tries to use global "six" instead of "six" module that is located under "botocore/vendored" directory.


Please, Could take a look on it?

python2.6
CentOS 5.4
botocore 1.5.39

Thanks

issue_title: unsupported markup: hash headers, code blocks, short refs
issue_body:
I have tried the recommonmark plugin locally and on RTFD.org, and it seems to fail fairly basic Commonmark markup:

http://monkeysign.readthedocs.io/en/latest/

Notice how the following tags are not parsed properly:

* `### Requirements` header
* code blocks (`apt-get install monkeysign` is rendered in a `<blockquote>`)
* links (`[OpenKeychain][]` and others)

All this works fine on the standard Commonmark tester here:

http://spec.commonmark.org/dingus/

[direct link](http://spec.commonmark.org/dingus/?text=Monkeysign%3A%20OpenPGP%20Key%20Exchange%20for%20Humans%0A%3D%3D%3D%3D%3D%3D%3D%3D%3D%3D%3D%3D%3D%3D%3D%3D%3D%3D%3D%3D%3D%3D%3D%3D%3D%3D%3D%3D%3D%3D%3D%3D%3D%3D%3D%3D%3D%3D%3D%3D%3D%3D%3D%0A%0Amonkeysign%20is%20a%20tool%20to%20overhaul%20the%20OpenPGP%20keysigning%20experience%20and%0Abring%20it%20closer%20to%20something%20that%20most%20primates%20can%20understand.%0A%0AThe%20project%20makes%20use%20of%20cheap%20digital%20cameras%20and%20the%20type%20of%20bar%0Acode%20known%20as%20a%20QRcode%20to%20provide%20a%20human-friendly%20yet%20still-secure%0Akeysigning%20experience.%0A%0ANo%20more%20reciting%20tedious%20strings%20of%20hexadecimal%20characters.%20And%2C%20you%0Acan%20build%20a%20little%20rogue%27s%20gallery%20of%20the%20people%20that%20you%20have%20met%20and%0Aexchanged%20keys%20with!%20(Well%2C%20not%20yet%2C%20but%20it%27s%20part%20of%20the%20plan.)%0A%0AMonkeysign%20also%20features%20a%20user-friendly%20commandline%20tool%2C%20similar%20to%0A%60caff%60%2C%20to%20sign%20OpenGPG%20keys%20following%20the%20current%20best%20practices.%0A%0AMonkeysign%20was%20written%20by%20Jerome%20Charaoui%20and%20Antoine%20Beaupr%C3%A9%20and%20is%0Alicensed%20under%20GPLv3.%0A%0AFeatures%0A---------%0A%0A%20*%20commandline%20and%20GUI%20interface%0A%20*%20GUI%20supports%20exchanging%20fingerprints%20with%20qrcodes%0A%20*%20print%20your%20OpenPGP%20fingerprint%20on%20a%20QRcode%0A%20*%20key%20signature%20done%20on%20a%20separate%20keyring%0A%20*%20signature%20sent%20in%20a%20crypted%20email%20to%20ensure%3A%0A%20%20%201.%20the%20signee%20controls%20the%20signed%20email%0A%20%20%202.%20the%20signee%20controls%20the%20private%20key%0A%20%20%203.%20the%20signee%20decides%20what%20to%20do%20with%20the%20signature%0A%20*%20local%20(%22non-exportable%22)%20signatures%0A%20*%20send%20through%20local%20email%20server%2C%20arbitrary%20SMTP%20server%20or%20other%0A%20%20%20programs%0A%0AInstalling%0A----------%0A%0AMonkeysign%20should%20be%20available%20in%20Debian%20and%20Ubuntu%2C%20but%20can%20also%0Aeasily%20be%20installed%20from%20source.%0A%0A%23%23%23%20Requirements%0A%0AThe%20following%20Python%20packages%20are%20required%20for%20the%20GUI%20to%20work.%0A%0A%20%20%20%20python-qrencode%20python-gtk2%20python-zbar%20python-zbarpygtk%0A%0AIf%20they%20are%20not%20available%2C%20the%20commandline%20signing%20tool%20should%20still%0Awork%20but%20doesn%27t%20recognize%20QR%20codes.%0A%0AOf%20course%2C%20all%20this%20depends%20on%20the%20GnuPG%20program.%0A%0A%23%23%23%20In%20Debian%20%2F%20Ubuntu%0A%0AMonkeysign%20is%20now%20in%20Debian%2C%20since%20Jessie%20(and%20backported%20to%20Wheezy)%0Aand%20Ubuntu%20(since%20Trusty%2014.04LTS).%20To%20install%20it%2C%20just%20run%3A%0A%0A%20%20%20%20apt-get%20install%20monkeysign%0A%0A%23%23%23%20From%20git%0A%0AYou%20can%20fetch%20monkeysign%20with%20git%3A%0A%0A%20%20%20%20git%20clone%20git%3A%2F%2Fgit.monkeysphere.info%2Fmonkeysign%0A%0A%23%23%23%20From%20source%0A%0AThe%20source%20tarball%20is%20also%20available%20directly%20from%20the%20Debian%20mirrors%0Ahere%3A%0A%0A%20%20%20%20http%3A%2F%2Fcdn.debian.net%2Fdebian%2Fpool%2Fmain%2Fm%2Fmonkeysign%2F%0A%0AThe%20%60.tar.gz%60%20file%20has%20a%20checksum%2C%20cryptographically%20signed%2C%20in%20the%0A%60.dsc%60%20file.%0A%0A%23%23%23%20Installing%20from%20source%0A%0ATo%20install%20monkeysign%2C%20run%3A%0A%0A%20%20%20%20sudo%20.%2Fsetup.py%20install%20--record%3Dinstall.log%0A%0ARunning%0A-------%0A%0AThe%20graphical%20interface%20should%20be%20self-explanatory%2C%20it%20should%20be%20in%0Ayour%20menus%20or%20call%20it%20with%3A%0A%0A%20%20%20%20monkeyscan%0A%0AThe%20commandline%20interface%20should%20provide%20you%20with%20a%20complete%20help%20file%0Awhen%20called%20with%20%60--help%60%3A%0A%0A%20%20%20%20monkeysign%20--help%0A%0AFor%20example%2C%20to%20sign%20a%20given%20fingerprint%3A%0A%0A%20%20%20%20monkeysign%2090ABCDEF1234567890ABCDEF1234567890ABCDEF%0A%0AThis%20will%20fetch%20the%20key%20from%20your%20keyring%20(or%20a%20keyserver)%20and%20sign%20it%0Ain%20a%20temporary%20keyring%2C%20then%20encrypt%20the%20signature%20and%20send%20it%20in%20an%0Aemail%20to%20the%20owner%20of%20the%20key.%0A%0ACaveats%0A-------%0A%0A%20*%20There%20are%20numerous%20bugs%20with%20odd%20keys%20and%20GnuPG%20corner%20cases.%20Most%0A%20%20%20of%20them%20should%20be%20documented%20on%20the%20Debian%20BTS%20here%3A%0A%20%20%20https%3A%2F%2Fbugs.debian.org%2Fmonkeysign%0A%0A%20*%20Running%20monkeysign%20in%20%60--debug%60%20mode%20and%20sending%20the%20output%20to%20a%0A%20%20%20public%20forum%20may%20leak%20public%20or%20even%20private%20key%20material%20in%20some%0A%20%20%20circumstances.%20Special%20efforts%20have%20been%20made%20so%20that%20private%20key%0A%20%20%20material%20is%20never%20output%20to%20the%20screen%2C%20but%20you%20can%20never%20be%20too%0A%20%20%20careful%3A%20look%20at%20the%20output%20before%20you%20send%20it.%0A%0A%20*%20The%20graphical%20interface%20isn%27t%20as%20complete%20as%20the%20commandline.%20A%20few%0A%20%20%20features%20are%20missing%20and%20it%20is%20mostly%20a%20proof%20of%20concept%20at%20this%0A%20%20%20point%20which%20works%2C%20but%20has%20a%20few%20rough%20edges.%20In%20particular%2C%20if%20you%0A%20%20%20have%20a%20high%20resolution%20camera%2C%20the%20camera%20window%20may%20fill%20your%0A%20%20%20screen%20completely%2C%20in%20which%20case%20you%20may%20want%20to%20change%20the%0A%20%20%20resolution%20beforehand%20using%20the%20following%20commands%3A%0A%0A%20%20%20%20%20%20%20%20sudo%20apt-get%20install%20v4l-utils%0A%20%20%20%20%20%20%20%20v4l2-ctl%20--set-fmt-video%3Dwidth%3D640%2Cheight%3D480%2Cpixelformat%3D1%0A%0A%20%20%20See%20https%3A%2F%2Fbugs.debian.org%2Fcgi-bin%2Fbugreport.cgi%3Fbug%3D723154%20for%0A%20%20%20more%20information%20about%20this%20specific%20problem.%0A%0A%20*%20Both%20the%20graphical%20and%20commandline%20interface%20assume%20you%20are%20fairly%0A%20%20%20familiar%20with%20SMTP%20configuration%20or%20had%20an%20administrator%20setup%20a%0A%20%20%20mailserver%20on%20your%20machine%20for%20email%20delivery.%0A%0ASupport%0A-------%0A%0ADiscussions%2C%20questions%20and%20issues%20can%20be%20brought%20up%20on%20the%20mailing%0Alist%2C%20%3Cmonkeysphere%40lists.riseup.net%3E.%20See%20the%20%5BCONTRIBUTING.md%5D%5B%5D%0Afile%20for%20more%20information%20about%20how%20to%20file%20bugs.%0A%0A%5BCONTRIBUTING.md%5D%3A%20CONTRIBUTING.md%0A%0ASimilar%20projects%0A----------------%0A%0A%20*%20%5BOpenKeychain%5D%5B%5D%2C%20a%20fork%20of%20%5BAPG%5D%5B%5D%2C%20has%20support%20for%20exporting%20and%0A%20%20%20importing%20fingerprints%20in%20QRcode%20and%20NFC.%20It%20uses%20similar%20strings%0A%20%20%20for%20QRcodes%20exchanges%20and%20is%20compatible%20with%0A%20%20%20Monkeysign.%20(%5BGithub%20project%5D%5B2%5D)%0A%0A%20%5BOpenKeychain%5D%3A%20https%3A%2F%2Fwww.openkeychain.org%2F%0A%20%5BAPG%5D%3A%20http%3A%2F%2Fwww.thialfihar.org%2Fprojects%2Fapg%2F%0A%20%5B2%5D%3A%20https%3A%2F%2Fgithub.com%2Fopen-keychain%2Fopen-keychain%0A%0A%20*%20%5BGPG%20for%20Android%5D%5B%5D%20(of%20the%20%5BGuardian%20project%5D%5B%5D)%20will%20import%0A%20%20%20public%20keys%20in%20your%20device%27s%20keyring%20when%20they%20are%20found%20in%0A%20%20%20QRcodes%2C%20so%20it%20should%20be%20able%20to%20talk%20with%20Monkeysign%2C%20but%20this%0A%20%20%20remains%20to%20be%20tested.%20(%5BGithub%20project%5D%5B%5D)%0A%20%0A%20%5BGPG%20for%20Android%5D%3A%20https%3A%2F%2Fguardianproject.info%2Fcode%2Fgnupg%2F%0A%20%5BGuardian%20project%5D%3A%20https%3A%2F%2Fguardianproject.info%2F%0A%20%5BGithub%20project%5D%3A%20https%3A%2F%2Fgithub.com%2Fguardianproject%2Fgnupg-for-android%0A%0A%20*%20%5BGibberbot%5D%5B%5D%20(also%20of%20the%20%5BGuardian%20project%5D%5B%5D)%20can%20exchange%20OTR%0A%20%20%20fingerprints%20using%20QRcodes.%20(%5BGithub%20project%5D%5B3%5D)%0A%0A%20%5BGibberbot%5D%3A%20https%3A%2F%2Fguardianproject.info%2Fapps%2Fgibber%2F%0A%20%5B3%5D%3A%20https%3A%2F%2Fgithub.com%2Fguardianproject%2FGibberbot%0A)

is this a bug in [commonmark.py](https://github.com/rtfd/CommonMark-py) or am i doing something wrong?

Bonjour,

Merci d'avoir accepté la Pull Request et fait le merge.

En revanche, vous n'avez pas encore mis à jour le paquet sur PyPi.

Cordialement,

issue_title: GitPython does not support N-way diffs.
issue_body:
Imagine I want to merge a feature branch into master, when that feature branch contains changes that conflict with master, as follows:
```
commit 87ef22e9631701222b8a461080484c1ee880c23c (HEAD -> master)
Merge: 895064f 54c3d06
Author: jose <jose@autonomic.ai>
Date:   Thu Feb 15 10:00:39 2018 -0500

    Merge branch 'feature2'

commit 895064f1ebcf08a6b031c23577e92ad16ee08abc
Author: jose <jose@autonomic.ai>
Date:   Thu Feb 15 09:59:47 2018 -0500

    Commit conflicting thing

diff --git a/example.txt b/example.txt
index 10d788f..7cd2517 100644
--- a/example.txt
+++ b/example.txt
@@ -1 +1 @@
-Initial content.
+This will conflict

commit 54c3d062efb24d8d591402f20d59f90183abb0ff (feature2)
Author: jose <jose@autonomic.ai>
Date:   Thu Feb 15 09:59:29 2018 -0500

    Commit my feature

diff --git a/example.txt b/example.txt
index 10d788f..23eb305 100644
--- a/example.txt
+++ b/example.txt
@@ -1 +1 @@
-Initial content.
+Feature feature feature 2.

commit 0c57867dbc6d27914eb03c3f3748d15d9e2565c8
Author: jose <jose@autonomic.ai>
Date:   Thu Feb 15 09:59:00 2018 -0500

    Initial commit

diff --git a/example.txt b/example.txt
new file mode 100644
index 0000000..10d788f
--- /dev/null
+++ b/example.txt
@@ -0,0 +1 @@
+Initial content.
```

In this case, in order to merge `feature2` into `master` I had to resolve a conflict; so the merge commit has some content *that is not in either of its parents*:

```
commit 87ef22e9631701222b8a461080484c1ee880c23c (HEAD -> master)
Merge: 895064f 54c3d06
Author: jose <jose@autonomic.ai>
Date:   Thu Feb 15 10:00:39 2018 -0500

    Merge branch 'feature2'

diff --cc example.txt
index 7cd2517,23eb305..e64dc83
--- a/example.txt
+++ b/example.txt
@@@ -1,1 -1,1 +1,1 @@@
- This will conflict
 -Feature feature feature 2.
++Resolving conflict: feature feature feature 2.
```

In git, I can use a three way diff as `git diff 87ef22e9631701222b8a461080484c1ee880c23c 87ef22e9631701222b8a461080484c1ee880c23c^1 87ef22e9631701222b8a461080484c1ee880c23c^2` to see only the changes that were made to resolve the merge conflict. (Incidentally this is equivalent to `git diff-tree --cc 87ef22e9631701222b8a461080484c1ee880c23c`,  and it's what `git show` uses internally)

Note that this differs from doing two separate `git diff`s against the individual parents of the commit. If I `git diff 87ef22e9631701222b8a461080484c1ee880c23c 87ef22e9631701222b8a461080484c1ee880c23c^1`, it will show not only the content brought in by resolving the merge commit, but also the content that was introduced by `87ef22e9631701222b8a461080484c1ee880c23c^2` that did not conflict with the first parent.

Currently GitPython does not support this sort of three-way (or n-way) diffing, so it is actually impossible to cleanly examine changes introduced by resolving merge conflicts.

issue_title: Add a parquet we use to control row-group sizes better
issue_body:
`parquet-hadoop` library does not support row-group sizes less then a 100 (PARQUET-409).
Until resolved by Parquet project, we should add a patch (or a reference to a pull request) + build instructions to make it easier for our users to generate parquet files with row groups smaller than a 100.

I've been trying to incorporate django-meta into a project. However, I running into an issue when putting namespaces into the head tag. Django 1.9 is raising an AttributeError `'dict' object has no attribute 'use_facebook'` at line 197 of `meta/templatetags/meta.py`.

I am having this problem in django-meta 1.2 and 1.3b3. Am I missing something important, or is django-meta failing to set the use_facebook attribute for the Meta class?

The top of my template looks like:
`<!DOCTYPE html>`
`<html>{% load staticfiles %}{% load meta %}`
`<head lang="en" {% meta_namespaces %}>`

In my settings I have:
`META_SITE_PROTOCOL = 'http'`
`META_USE_TITLE_TAG=True`
`META_SITE_NAME = "Site name"`
`META_SITE_DOMAIN = 'Site domain'`
`META_USE_OG_PROPERTIES = True`
`META_USE_FACEBOOK_PROPERTIES = True`

In my view, the context dictionary is:
`    context_dict['meta'] = {`
`        'use_title_tag':True,`
`        'use_facebook':True,`
`        'title':...`
`    }`

**Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.**
>_"Okay, good to know. Is there any public place where that discussion and future ones can be viewed?"_
https://github.com/pypa/pipenv/issues/2191#issuecomment-391479745

**Describe the solution you'd like**
Would it be possible to get some extra reporting on decisions that are likely to impact people like this? I'm really not bothered by the versionning system change but it was a bit disappointing neither /releases or `HISTORY.txt` have anything notable about the change. `HISTORY` just had a single line of `- Switch to calver for versioning.` and I had to search the issue tracker to find the perfectly valid reasoning. 

pipenv is becoming increasingly important to _my_ projects so a bit more overt transparency would be appreciated even if _my_ projects aren't part of pipenv :wink: 

**Describe alternatives you've considered**
Slap a bit more information in `HISTORY.txt` when there is something worth talking about. Sure, "worth" is a very subjective concept but there is no reason why `HISTORY.txt` can't be ~rewritten~ updated if people later notice that there was something important to talk about.

Actually, /releases and `HISTORY.txt` are just the first places I would check. Even slapping in a link to a blog post somewhere is fine.

Locking can be very slow on some machines - for example, I always run pipenv with the `--skip-lock` option.

It would be useful to control that behaviour from an environment variable.

issue_title: do not re-lock dependencies that are already locked when doing a pipenv install
issue_body:
Do not re-lock dependencies that are already locked when doing a `pipenv install <package>`. If I have a lockfile and want to add a new package, there does not seem to be a sane way to add that package without triggering pipenv to upgrade the locked versions of all installed packages.

##### Describe your environment

1. OS Type: `macos 10.13.3`
2. Python version: `Python 3.6.0`
3. Pipenv version: `pipenv, version 9.0.3`

##### Expected result

Run `pipenv install pkgA` and then later `pipenv install pkgB` and see that `pkgA` was untouched, still pinned where it was before.

##### Actual result

Run `pipenv install pkgA` and then later `pipenv install pkgB` and see that pkgA was upgraded to a newer version just because I installed unrelated `pkgB`.

In yarn this is very clearly separated by `yarn install` versus `yarn upgrade` where it will only re-lock the dependency if you change its pin range and run `yarn install` or if you run `yarn upgrade`. If you run `yarn install` without changing the pin on the dependency then it is never upgraded.

Hello,
I am having some trouble with what I believe to be a rather simple pykafka use case.

I have set up a loop that goes on retrieving messages from a topic (one producer, one partition, no replication, only one consumer having exclusive access to the server), but sometimes it sort of gets stuck in a loop.
I have wrapped the usage of pykafka within a class of my own that, among other things, prints the offset of the retrieved message along with the first characters of its body and from time to time the offset coming out are arranged in an endlessly repeating sequence like:
 ...
 99
100
101
102
103
104
101 ( x )
102
103
104
101 ( x )
102
103
104
102 ( xx )
103
104
101 ( xxx )
102
103
104
 ...

(I omit the text of the messages, they correspond 1:1 to the offsets).
(In real cases the loop has an extension up to few hundreds of messages. My current offsets are in the half-millions range).
This happens regardless of whether I manually invoke commit_offsets() or set auto commit with a few seconds interval; the exact offset where the sequence restarts is not always the same (e.g. above it restarts from 101 in places marked x and xxx but from 102 in xx ).

I believe this is a very strange behaviour since it forces me to manually store the highest offset seen so far and check against it: whenever I detect this infinite looping behaviour, since commits are not effective, I have to stop(), wait some seconds, and start() again the simple_consumer.

I noticed this tends to happen (but perhaps not exclusively) when the consumer has reached the current end of the topic: however, if new messages are produced to it when the consumer is looping, this is not sufficient to make it exit towards the fresh messages.

Here are the kafka_server/pykafka/python versions I tested this on:
* kafka server version 0.9.0.1
* Python 2.7.10 (default, Oct 14 2015, 16:09:02)  [GCC 5.2.1 20151010] on linux2
* pykafka==2.2.1

I provide below what I deem the relevant parts of the class that provides an interface between pykafka and my other code. I'd be glad if someone can point me to the right direction -- it might well be that this is indeed the desired behaviour, in which case I may just need to rethink some parts of my code.

CLASS CODE:
```python
from pykafka import KafkaClient
kserver_default_string='RE.DA.CT.ED:port'

socket_timeout_ms=30000
offsets_channel_socket_timeout_ms=10000

class kafka_agent:

    def __init__(self, topic, kserver=ks_default_string, consumer_timeout_ms=350):
        self.topic=topic
        self.kserver=kserver
        self.kclient=KafkaClient(hosts=kserver,socket_timeout_ms=socket_timeout_ms,offsets_channel_socket_timeout_ms=offsets_channel_socket_timeout_ms)
        self.ktopic=self.kclient.topics[topic]
        self.kconsumer=self.ktopic.get_simple_consumer(consumer_group='cgroup_'+topic,
            consumer_timeout_ms=consumer_timeout_ms,auto_commit_enable=True, auto_commit_interval_ms=10000)

    def retrieve(self, block=True):
        if not self.kconsumer._running:
            self.kconsumer.start()
        tmsg=self.kconsumer.consume(block=block)
        if tmsg is not None:
            tstring=tmsg.value
            toff=tmsg.offset
            return tstring
        else:
            return None

    def commit_last_retrieve(self):
        self.kconsumer.commit_offsets()

    def isrunning(self):
        return self.kconsumer._running

    # those are for usage by context managers
    def __exit__(self, exc_type, exc_value, traceback):
        self.kconsumer.stop()

    def __enter__(self):
        self.kconsumer.start()
```

USER CODE: something along the lines of:
```python

with kafka_agent('TOPIC_NAME') as kagent:
    while True:
        obj=kagent.retrieve()
        if obj is not None:
            # do stuff with obj
        else:
            # None was retrieved
        kagent.commit_last_retrieve()
```

===



Thanks a lot!

This is a question rather a problem. 

I had a situation which required a slightly different approach to the HashingPartitioner, and I wondered if it's worth contributing to the main project?

My particular use case sometimes causes many incoming messages have the same partitioning key. This means partitions can be temporarily overloaded, because the HashingPartitioner will (correctly) assign messages with the same key to the same partition. 

To overcome this, I specialized the HashingPartitioner to divide the incoming messages between a configurable subset of the available partitions. For example, given a total of sixteen partitions, one could specify a group size of four to smooth the distribution of incoming messages across a group of four partitions, thus avoiding hot spots in one partition. 

Essentially this is a modification of this line of the HashingPartitioner: https://github.com/Parsely/pykafka/blob/master/pykafka/partitioners.py#L82
with this line:
```return partitions[abs(self.hash_func(key) + randrange(0, self.group_size)) % len(partitions)]```

Please let me know if you think this is more widely useful and I will work on a pull request.

issue_title: Consider switching FWSerializable to monty serialization

issue_title: x509 module with Rest Framework: UNIQUE constraint failed
issue_body:
I have discovered a really strange problem with django-x509. I have created a small demo application to trigger that problem:

https://github.com/hggh/certrest

If you want to create a certificate via the REST API the cert itself is created at the database but the REST Framework throws a IntegrityError.

Steps:

1. git clone https://github.com/hggh/certrest
2. pip install -r requirements.txt
3. ./manage migrate
4. ./mange createsuperuser
5. create a new CA inside the admin: http://127.0.0.1:8000/admin/certs/ca/add/
6. http://127.0.0.1:8000/certs/certs/
7. create a new certificate with the default CreateView in Django: http://127.0.0.1:8000/certs/certs/create/
7.1 this works great the certificate is created and will be shown inside the Listview
8. Goto the REST Framework: http://127.0.0.1:8000/certs/api/cert/
9. create a new certificate within the REST Framework View
10. click on "POST"
11. Django throws: "UNIQUE constraint failed: certs_cert.id"

My conclusion is that the Serializer do something different to the ModelForm.
Review the section and ensure it still makes sense and works

issue_title: oauthclient: validate email address on user sign-up
issue_body:
For oauth services were the email address is not provided upon first sign up (such as ORCID) we currently show a sign-up form with an input for the email address but that email address is not being validated.

The email address should be validated but ideally the flow of the user wanting, for example, to submit should not be blocked until he/she validates email address.

So the proposal would be to do something similar to what https://balsamiq.com/ is doing. Upon sign-up you can start using the app right away but the validation email states:

> To start your trial, you need to verify your email address. Please go to the link below and log in:

> https://inspirehep.mybalsamiq.com/confirm/ac62ef1a8ce02277c901b61d86690e7fb3d1e1fb

> If you don't confirm within the next 2 days, your site will be suspended. If you don't confirm within a month, the site will be deleted.

> Almost there!

So we could allow for some days to access without validation and after that, block the account.

issue_title: api: patch record not behaving as expected
issue_body:
Record.patch currently returns a patched data object, but has no impact on the database record. This is misleading as far as an API call goes. 

I would propose this (open of course to suggestions for improvements) 

``` python
def patch(self, patch):
        model = self.model
        data = apply_patch(dict(self), patch)
        # perform the commit on the patched data.

        try:
            patched_record = self.__class__(data, model=model)
            patched_record.commit()
            return patched_record
        except Exception:
            raise
```

It would also be good to perhaps return back an updated version of the Record object rather than a new instance.

issue_title: Field zope.schema.Object does not check for invariants
issue_body:
In https://bugs.launchpad.net/zope.schema/+bug/567520, @andreypopp reported:

> Field zope.schema.Object does not check invariant during validation. I have attached simple test for that.

and attached a file:  https://bugs.launchpad.net/zope.schema/+bug/567520/+attachment/1341035/+files/test_object_invariants_bug.py

and a patch: https://bugs.launchpad.net/zope.schema/+bug/567520/+attachment/1341770/+files/object_invariants.patch

@tseaver followed up:

> The issue is definitely present: Object fields do not enforce their schema invariants.
> 
> I have reviewed the patch, and like the idea. I am concerned that obscuring the Invalid eror raised by the invariant check is going to lead to hard-to-debug errors, so I haven't committed the patch. Instead, I am assigning the Christian for confirmation.

@andreypopp replied:

> Just for some clarification, I'm wrapping exceptions raised during Object field's invariant check to differentiate them later from exceptions raised by other fields.

`fire.Fire` looked like a very interesting function so I wanted to inspect it with `fire.Fire`. I was surprised that the interpreter printed out a dictionary with all my locals and globals.

issue_title: Ability to avoid rare segmentation causing UNKs
issue_body:
When training a joint SPM model on two or more languages, is there a way to alleviate the problem of segmenting a token in language1 into subunits seen in language2 causing UNKs during test-time?

In subword-nmt, there's a vocabulary threshold for this that allows further segmentation of tokens until the subunits are seen with at least that threshold times in the relevant language.

issue_title: Using results of Poisson CCD Simulator to simulate BF effect
issue_body: Craig Lage at UC Davis has developed a Poisson equation solver that computes the distortion of the pixel edges as the electrons get collected in the CCD pixels. In the DESC Hack day today, we made some progress towards integrating his code (in C++) with the C++ layer of the GalSim. We identified  that the natural way to include this functionality would be by tweaking the photon-shooting method inside the `drawImage` routine.  I will push the code after some cleanup, but this is not completely done yet.

It took on my laptop 28min to plot 120 figures (~14s per plot) with the following code:

```
  nrn = load_neuron(morph_name, release_path)
  bb = geom.bounding_box(nrn)
  max_projection = np.max(np.abs(list(bb[0][:2]) + list(bb[1][:2])))
  center = nrn.soma.center[:2]
  view.neuron(nrn, output_path=output_path, output_name=morph_name,
                    xlim=[center[0] - max_projection, center[0] + max_projection],
                    ylim=[center[1] - max_projection, center[1] + max_projection],
                    dpi=275)
```
I think it is way too slow.

issue_title: Disconnecting while MQTTClient.deliver_message() is pending raises IndexError
issue_body:
If the broker disconnects while `MQTTClient.deliver_message()` is pending and `auto_reconnect` is turned off (and presumably also if `auto_reconnect` fails, but I haven't tested that), `deliver_message()` will raise an `IndexError`:

    WARNING:hbmqtt.client:Disconnected from broker
    DEBUG:transitions.core:Initiating transition from state connected to state disconnected...
    DEBUG:transitions.core:Exiting state connected. Processing callbacks...
    INFO:transitions.core:Exited state connected
    DEBUG:transitions.core:Entering state disconnected. Processing callbacks...
    INFO:transitions.core:Entered state disconnected
    ERROR:asyncio:Task exception was never retrieved
    future: <Task finished coro=<mqtt_loop() done, defined at ./foo.py:19> exception=IndexError('pop from an empty deque',)>
    Traceback (most recent call last):
      File "./foo.py", line 25, in mqtt_loop
        message = await mqtt.deliver_message()
      File "/usr/local/lib/python3.6/site-packages/hbmqtt/client.py", line 334, in deliver_message
        self.client_tasks.pop()
    IndexError: pop from an empty deque

This happens because `cancel_tasks()` called by `handle_connection_close()` pops all tasks from `self.client_tasks` as it cancels them. When the `deliver_task` is cancelled, `deliver_message()` processes it as if it finished normally and tries to pop it from `self.client_tasks` again, resulting in the exception.

I'm not sure what the intended behavior here is. From a user perspective, I think it'd be most useful if `deliver_message()` would just pass on the `ClientException("Connection lost")` from `cancel_tasks()`.
