issue_body:
I'm trying the ubuntu 17.10 to see how it will go when released.

What I have found is when one of the built-in gnome apps (at least those) in focus - the guake's global shortcut does not work. So if you open any gnome app that is pre-installed, eg: calculator, updates settings, settings, gnome terminal, etc: then the global shortcut does not toggle the guake.

3rd party apps though, like firefox don't intercept the shortcut.

It does not matter what exact shortcut is set up to toggle the guake: either the default `F12` or something exotic like `alt+F12` or `ctrl+alt+~` - the gnome apps intercept any of those anyway.

Is it a known problem? Any solution so far?

Steps to reproduce:

1. Install the ubuntu 17.10 beta2 (you may or may not upgrade all the packages, it does not change anything)
1. Install guake
1. Start guake
1. Start calculator and firefox
1. Focus firefox and press F12. See the guake was shown
1. Focus calculator and press F12. Guake does not respond

I've just run into a problem where we use environment variables in our include paths, like so:
```
include_directories(
  ${catkin_INCLUDE_DIRS}
  $ENV{dependency_ROOT}/include
  )
```

This leads to the following error:
```
error: build include path '$ENV{dependency_ROOT}/include' does not exist
```
Now, I'm aware the use of environment variables is strongly discouraged, but still, the path pointed to by this *does* exist.

I'm ready to put together a Pull Request that implements environment variable expansion, but I wanted to discuss if and where to make this change.

To me it seems like there are two obvious places to resolve environment variables: ```real_path()``` (in linter.py) and ```_resolve_vars()``` (in cmake.py).

I think changing ```_resolve_vars()``` (or adding an equivalent ```_resolve_env_vars()``` function makes the most sense.
Changing the behavior of ```_resolve_vars()``` would change the outcome of ```test_substitution()```, like so:
```
self.assertEqual(
    self.parse_all("cmd($ENV{PATH})"),
    [ ("cmd", [ "$ENV{PATH}" ]) ]
)
```
would become
```
self.assertEqual(
    self.parse_all("cmd($ENV{PATH})"),
    [ ("cmd", [ os.path.expandvars('${PATH}' ]) ]
)
```

One argument for changing ```real_path()``` instead is that linter.py already imports os and uses it heavily, whereas cmake.py does not.

What do you think?

issue_title: Datastore: Add support for new 'TransactionOptions' field
issue_body:
## Overview

Datastore has introduced two transaction options that can be passed when creating a transaction (BeginTransaction RPC):
- A transaction can be declared read-only. A read-only transaction cannot modify data.
- A read-write transaction can provide the ID of a previous transaction.

## Rationale

### Transaction Mode

Transactions can optionally be created in read-only or read-write mode. Specifying the mode of the transaction will allow for Datastore to perform future performance optimizations in upcoming changes.

A read-write transaction can read and modify entities. Read-write transactions that operate on the same data can conflict and suffer from reduced throughput due to contention.

A read-only transaction cannot modify entities; in return they do not contend with other read-write or read-only transactions. Using a read-only transaction for transactions that only read data will potentially improve throughput.

### Transaction Retry

Read-write transactions may fail due to contention. A read-write transaction can be retried by specifying `previous_transaction` when creating the new transaction. 

Specifying `previous_transaction` provides more information that can be used to improve throughput. In particular, if transactional operations `A` and `B` conflict, specifying the `previous_transaction` can help to prevent livelock.  

## Requirements

Users should be able to specify that a transaction is read-only when they create it.
When retrying a read-write transaction, the previous transaction’s ID should be included in the request.
If your client has a `RunInTransaction` method that takes a function and automatically creates and retries the transaction, then this method should handle setting the previous transaction ID. There is no need to expose the option to the user.
If your client requires that users retry themselves, then expose the option.

## Suggested Test Plan

### Unit Tests

- When the read-only option is passed, the BeginTransactionRequest is
        project_id:"project" transaction_options:<read_only:<> > 
- When the previous transaction ID “tid” is passed, the BeginTransactionRequest is
project_id:"project"
    transaction_options:<read_write:<previous_transaction:"tid" > > 
- If you have a `RunInTransaction function, simulate a retryable failure to verify that previous_transaction is passed on retries.

### Integration Tests

- A read-only transaction can perform reads.
- A read-only transaction fails if it attempts to write. This test currently fails against the production endpoint.

## Resources

- [Public protos](https://github.com/googleapis/googleapis/blob/master/google/datastore/v1/datastore.proto#L352)
- [Go implementation](https://code-review.googlesource.com/c/gocloud/+/18350)
- [C# implementation](https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-dotnet/pull/1447)

issue_title: bundle_create: if os.symlink fails, more informative error message, please
issue_body:
A lovely person ran './pants goal bundle something' and got a stack trace and exception ending in...

> os.symlink(path, os.path.join(lib_dir, external_jar))
> OSError: [Errno 17] File exists

Troubleshooting probably would have been easier if instead of the opaque 'File exists', we had something more like 'File exists, specifically {{}}'.format(os.path.join(lib_dir, external_jar))

A few ideas stumbled upon while trying to speed up CI burns on #6104:
+ Cache src/rust/engine/target in .travis.yml
   The official [rust language support](https://docs.travis-ci.com/user/languages/rust/#Dependency-Management) in Travis caches [`~/.cargo` and `target/`](https://github.com/travis-ci/travis-ci/issues/4470), so this is kosher from their point of view and blessed by Chrichton. I tried this already and the upshot is all 3rdparty crate compilations are cached when they have not changed versions - easy win.
+ Throttle `cargo build` as we do for zinc in pants.travis-ci.ini
   This can be done via `CARGO_BUILD_JOBS=N` in .travis.yml and might give us better throughput with less thrash. Measurements needed.
+ Throttle `cargo test` similar to above.
   Not sure how to best do this. There is rumor `RUST_TEST_THREADS=N` works, though it looks discouraged. I've had great luck with [`cargo make ...`](https://crates.io/crates/cargo-make) which allows setting up arbitrary commands in a `Makefile.toml` on a side project. Might be a way to go for these bottom two bullets. Likely very little bang for the buck here though, tests are still pretty fast.

issue_title: Can implicit API-Gateway definition from Lambda function definition have custom authorizer?
issue_body:
If I'd like to define my API-Gateway through an 'implicit way', i.e., by defining the Lambda function and its associated Events-->ApiEventSource. See an example below.

Could I define the custom authorizer in this way? If so, how?



(Here is an example copied&pasted from https://github.com/awslabs/serverless-application-model/issues/104)
```
Resources:
  Create:
    Type: AWS::Serverless::Function
    Properties:
      Handler: meta/create.create
      CodeUri: app/
      Runtime: nodejs4.3
      Policies: AWSLambdaBasicExecutionRole
      Environment:
        Variables:
          DYNAMODB_TABLE_ENV: !Sub '${ServiceName}-${Stage}'
      Events:
        ApiEventSource:
          Type: Api
          Properties:
            Path: /
            Method: POST

  Delete:
    Type: AWS::Serverless::Function
    Properties:
      Handler: meta/delete.delete
      CodeUri: app/
      Runtime: nodejs4.3
      Policies: AWSLambdaBasicExecutionRole
      Environment:
        Variables:
          DYNAMODB_TABLE_ENV: !Sub '${ServiceName}-${Stage}'
      Events:
        ApiEventSource:
          Type: Api
          Properties:
            Path: /meta/{metaId}
            Method: DELETE
```

I was using a fork that had HTML tags so I wrote a test script, downloaded the most recent version of python-docx and I tried this on both ubuntu and windows machines running python2.7.

issue_title: map API data to vuex state
issue_body:
Leftover task from #1257 

Currently when displaying learner/exercise detail reports, data from the server is dropped directly into vuex without an intermediary mapping state which explicitly defines which attributes are extracted and where they're stored. This means that state is implicitly defined in vue files, and we end up with snake_case attributes (e.g. `exercise.extra_fields`).

We should add the appropriate state mappers in coach reports actions.

issue_title: BaseEstimator.state_counts() erroneously reports zero for nodes with a single parent
issue_body:
### Subject of the issue
When computing state counts for nodes with a single parent, the function `BaseEstimator.state_counts()` erroneously reports zero.

### Your environment
* pgmpy version: commit [f9be170]; also reproduced in 0.1.6
* Python version: Python 3.6.4
* Operating System: macOS High Sierra (10.13.3)

### Steps to reproduce
```python
import pandas as pd

from pgmpy.estimators import BaseEstimator

data = pd.DataFrame(data={'fruit': ["banana", "apple", "banana", "apple", "banana","apple", "banana", 
                                    "apple", "apple", "apple", "banana", "banana", "apple", "banana",], 
                          'tasty': ["yes", "no", "yes", "yes", "yes", "yes", "yes", 
                                    "yes", "yes", "yes", "yes", "no", "no", "no"], 
                          'size': ["large", "large", "large", "small", "large", "large", "large",
                                    "small", "large", "large", "large", "large", "small", "small"]})

be = BaseEstimator(data)
print(be.state_counts('tasty', parents=['fruit']))  # conditional on fruit
```

### Expected behaviour
```
fruit  apple  banana
tasty               
no         2       2
yes        5       5
```

### Actual behaviour
```
 fruit apple banana
tasty             
no      0.0    0.0
yes     0.0    0.0
```

issue_title: Can we combine Attention and MatrixAttention?
issue_body: It should be pretty easy to just have a single `Attention` class, instead of parallel `Attention` and `MatrixAttention` classes - just use the code for `MatrixAttention`, and `unsqueeze(1)` and re-`squeeze(1)` if we're given a single vector.  Is there a problem with this I'm not seeing?  I'm not sure why we didn't just do this to begin with...  Maybe because of how Keras shape inference worked...?

issue_title: Potential file descriptor/memory leak in deis-logger
issue_body:
When deis-logger can't create or write to its log files, it seems to make runaway attempts to open the file numerous times per second. We have anecdotal evidence that this leads to high load and memory usage on the CoreOS host, leading to components crashing/restarting and having other oddities.

See https://github.com/deis/deis/issues/2372#issuecomment-61867924 for details.

issue_title: add CLI unit tests for lowercase app name
issue_body: waiting on client unit tests. #2192

This proposal would allow people to use builder without installing buildpacks.

Why do this?

Primarily because buildpacks are huge. If only you use dockerfile based apps, disabling buildpacks saves startup time and diskspace.


How would this work?

There would be a etcd key called `/deis/builder/disableBuildpacks`. If that is set, builder would skip buildpack setup and regect pushes for apps without `Dockerfiles`.

Migration?

When upgrading the cluster the user could do nothing or set the ETCD key to disable buildpacks for the new release.

Testing?

This would be tested by builder unit and functional tests.

issue_title: Should zero-length tuple types be a valid type?
issue_body:
*Note: This issue ticket doesn't follow the usual format because I couldn't think of a way to fit it into that.*

In the [solidity ABI spec](https://solidity.readthedocs.io/en/develop/abi-spec.html#types), it appears that zero-length tuple types are a valid type:

> (T1,T2,...,Tn): tuple consisting of the types T1, …, Tn, n >= 0

If that was intentional, then should it be assumed that this is some kind of [unit type](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Unit_type) (e.g. like `None` in Python or `()` in Haskell)?

Additionally, if it's correct to see zero-length tuples as unit types, how should we support that?  I suppose an encoder for the unit type could just encode `b'\x00'` in every case and just ignore whatever Python value it is given to encode.  A decoder could also just return `None` by default in every case for the decoded value but still consume and ignore 256 bits of data.

```
inbam = pysam.AlignmentFile("test/test_data/small.lmna.bam", "rb")
pairedreads = pysam.AlignmentFile("allpaired.bam", "wb", template=inbam)
for read in inbam.fetch():
     if read.is_paired:
             pairedreads.write(read)

pairedreads.close()
inbam.close()
```

...this works as expected and creates a correctly formatted BAM file.  However, replacing the second line with
```
pairedreads = pysam.AlignmentFile("allpaired.sam", "w", template=inbam)
```
results in a file with no header and just the reads. 

Seems a bit inconsistent, but perhaps there's a good reason for it?

issue_title: How to interpret HMM states without labeling?
issue_body:
First of all, thanks for sharing the work on HMM, it's really usefull. 

My question is as follows. I have a data set which consists of positive floating-point numbers: `X = [x1, x2, ..., xn]`. Then I use this array to train a GMMHMM model. 

`gmmhmm_model = hmmlearn.hmm.GMMHMM(n_components=2, n_mix=3, covariance_type="full", algorithm="viterbi", init_params="stcmw", params="stcmw", n_iter=20, tol=1e-4)`

```
X = numpy.array(X).reshape(-1, 1)
gmmhmm_model.fit(X, length=[X.shape[0]])
```

Then, I use predict function to get a sequence of states given test data Y which is a set of positive floating-point numbers like X. 

```
Y = numpy.array(Y).reshape(-1, 1)
seq = gmmhmm_model.predict(Y, lengths=[Y.shape[0]])
```

I will get a sequence of 0s and 1s which correspond to the two HMM states I defined. But my question is how do you interpret these states as you don't use labels when training. Can I say, state_0 always represents small numbers and vice versa?

issue_title: How can I set the event loop's task factory using the pytest plugin?
issue_body:
## Long story short

I have introduced `aiotask_context` to an existing project in order to implement something like a request context in aiohttp using the middleware feature, which then enabled me to scope SQLAlchemy sessions to the request by using the `scopedsession` factory class. However, doing so made all the aiohttp pytest tests to fail, which was expected but I don't know how to fix them properly as the docs are not clear regarding the event loop (fixtures?) and how to configure it. The application itself runs without any errors when tested manually, the tests however are failing.

## Expected behaviour

All tests passing using the client fixture:

```python
import pytest

from example.app import create_app


@pytest.fixture
async def client(aiohttp_client):
    app = create_app()
    return await aiohttp_client(app)
```

Example test:

```python
import pytest
from tests.common import PING_API_URL


@pytest.mark.usefixtures("client")
class TestGet:
    async def test_get_healthy(self, client):
        response = await client.get(PING_API_URL)
        assert response.status == 200
        text = await response.text()
        assert text == '"pong"'
```

## Actual behaviour

This is the error I get when run the tests:

```
  File "/path/to/Workspace/example-app/example/middlewares.py", line 89, in middleware
    context.set("X-Request-ID", request.headers.get("X-Request-ID", str(uuid.uuid4())))
  File "path/to/Workspace/example-app/.testing-venv/lib/python3.6/site-packages/aiotask_context/__init__.py", line 115, in set
    asyncio.Task.current_task().context[key] = value
AttributeError: '_asyncio.Task' object has no attribute 'context'
```

## Steps to reproduce

Implement the following middleware and database sessions scoping function for your SQLAlchemy Session factory class:

```python
import asyncio
import aiotask_context as context
from sqlalchemy.orm import sessionmaker, scoped_session


###
# Middleware
#
async def request_id_middleware(app, handler):
    async def middleware_handler(request):
        context.set("X-Request-ID", request.headers.get("X-Request-ID", str(uuid.uuid4())))
        response = await handler(request)
        response.headers["X-Request-ID"] = context.get("X-Request-ID")
        return response
    return middleware_handler


###
# Scoping function
#
def get_current_request() -> Optional[str]:
    return context.get("X-Request-ID", None)


###
# Application
#
_app = web.Application(
    logger=_logger,
    middlewares=(
        request_id_middleware,
    ),
)


###
# Database w/ SQLAlchemy
#
_db_engine = _create_database_engine()
Session = scoped_session(
    sessionmaker(bind=_db_engine),
    scopefunc=get_current_request)


###
# Running the application
#
loop = asyncio.get_event_loop()
loop.set_task_factory(context.task_factory)

app = create_app()

web.run_app(app=app, host="0.0.0.0", port=8080)
```

## Your environment

 * Ubuntu 16.04
 * Pyenv
 * Python v3.6.3
 * Aiohttp v3.1.0
 * aiotask_context v0.5.0
 * SQLAlchemy v1.2.5

issue_title: XHR in notebook server backend for some browsers
issue_body:
While developing I hit some issue with some browsers when using notebooks through the following setup:

* proxy NGINX before JupyterHub
* JupyterHub spawns Jupyter Labs (dockerspawner)
* using Jupyter Lab UI in the browser

In Chromium and its derivates such as Chrome, I would not be able to open any notebooks in the frontend, since the notebook server refused the XHR request, while that worked fine in Firefox and its derivate Waterfox. Browser versions were:

* Waterfox 56.0.1 (64-bit)
* Firefox: ??? (something modern with Quantum)
* Chromium: Version 63.0.3239.84 (Official Build) Built on Ubuntu , running on Ubuntu 17.10 (64-bit)
* Chrome Version 64.0.3282.85 (Official Build) beta (64-bit)

The notebook package version is: `5.2.2`.

In the end it turned out, that the difference between their requests was, that Firefox and Waterfox do not set the `origin` header field, probably for privacy reasons, while Chromium and its derivate Chrome set it.

In the code of the notebook server in `notebook/base/handlers.py` there are three checks which allow the method `check_origin` to `return True`, which will allow the XHR to be accepted by the notebook server. FF and WF will exit the method because `origin` is not set in their requests, while Chromium and Chrome have it set and proceed to the next check.

Now because the whole thing is behind an NGINX, the NGINX needs to be configured explicitly to set the `Host` header field, otherwise it discards that, which will lead to Chromium and Chrome XHRs being refused / blocked, which is logged in the backend:

```
[W 2018-01-23 14:52:03.510 admin handlers:360] Blocking Cross Origin API request for /user/admin/api/sessions.  Origin: https://lab.localhost.com, Host: 172.18.0.3:8000
[W 2018-01-23 14:52:03.511 admin handlers:479] Not Found
```

The relevant routes for which the `Host` field needs to be set afaik are:

* `/user`
* `/` OR (`~* /api/sessions/?` AND `~* /api/contents/?`)
* `~* /(api/kernels/[^/]+/(channels|iopub|shell|stdin)|terminals/websocket)/?`

Of course these routes could be split into other partitions than they are in this list. The regular expression could differ etc.

If I am missing some crucial information to answer this issue, let me know here and hopefully I can provide it.

Execute http://mybinder.org/repo/BruceSherwood/vpython-jupyter and choose a demo program. You get "Kernel not found" and "I couldn't find a kernel matching VPython. Please select a kernel:" The pull-down menu in fact lists VPython, and after choosing "VPython" the program can run. Though I first saw this problem in the Binder context, later I've seen it on some machines running locally. Presumably the notebook contains the information that the VPython kernel is needed, and it's clear that this notebook is indeed available (installing vpython also installs vpnotebook).

For a tool dependency definition there should be only two files present, ``.shed.yml`` and ``tool_dependencies.xml`` (since the Tool Shed insists on just the ``tool_dependencies.xml`` being uploaded, not even allowing a README file).

```
$ more galaxy_blast/dependencies/package_blast_plus_2_2_30/.shed.yml
name: package_blast_plus_2_2_30
owner: iuc
homepage_url: https://github.com/peterjc/galaxy_blast/dependencies/package_blast_plus_2_2_30
remote_repository_url: https://github.com/peterjc/galaxy_blast/dependencies/package_blast_plus_2_2_30
description: NCBI BLAST+ 2.2.30 (binaries only) 
long_description: |
  This Tool Shed package is intended to be used as a dependency of the Galaxy wrappers
  for NCBI BLAST+ and any other tools which call the BLAST+ binaries internally.

  Development is hosted on GitHub https://github.com/peterjc/galaxy_blast/ which can
  be used for reporting any issues.
categories:
- Sequence Analysis
type: tool_dependency_definition
```

```
$ planemo shed_lint galaxy_blast/dependencies/package_blast_plus_2_2_30
Linting repository /mnt/galaxy/repositories/galaxy_blast/dependencies/package_blast_plus_2_2_30
Applying linter dependencies... CHECK
.. INFO: tool_dependencies.xml found and appears to be valid XML
Applying linter itory_dependencies... CHECK
.. INFO: No repository_dependencies.xml, skipping.
Applying linter yaml... CHECK
.. INFO: .shed.yml found and appears to be valid YAML.
Applying linter e... CHECK
.. INFO: No README found skipping.
```

Having looked at the ``.shed.yml`` and noticed ``type: tool_dependency_definition``, rather than looking at any ``repository_dependencies.xml`` this should be checking there isn't such a file.

Having a README file would be harmless (not used by the ToolShed, but might be useful for humans, and would be shown on GitHub/Bitbucket etc).

issue_title: Add post-sync build directive for modules
issue_body:
Currently if a module's source requires a build process to compile after syncing that's more involved than picking or dropping certain files, said process needs to be run after a `peru sync`. 

The feature would be specifying in `peru.yaml` a script within the module to execute after a `peru sync`. Perhaps use keyword `build`.

It's possible to script this in a watcher that wraps `peru` sync operations, so maybe this is out of scope for the project, similar to `peru override watch` #163. (?)

issue_title: Discovery importing dependent modules multiple times
issue_body:

Work around is to import the modules from the hooks/ stub.

    return list(tokenize_generator(sequence, print_function))
  File "/Users/andersh/Projects/mutmut/env/lib/python2.7/site-packages/baron/tokenizer.py", line 114, in tokenize_generator
    raise UnknowItem("Can't find a matching token for this item: '%s'" % item)
baron.tokenizer.UnknowItem: Can't find a matching token for this item: 'e-1'
```

The `read` method has this signature:

```python
def read(self, entity=None, attrs=None, ignore=()):
```

The `ignore` argument is a tuple of field names that NailGun should not try to populate when reading data from the server. For example, this is the signature of `DockerComputeResource.read`:

```python
def read(self, entity=None, attrs=None, ignore=('password',)):
```

This method signature works well if one wants to ignore a certain method attribute at all times. However, this method signature does not work well if one wants to conditionally ignore some field due to a bug. For example, this bit of code is used in response to a specific bug:

```python
ignore = set(ignore).union([…])  # list comprehension
if 'errata_id' in attrs:
    ignore.discard('errata')  # pylint:disable=no-member
ignore = tuple(ignore)
```

In the above code snippet, `ignore` is converted to a set, manipulated, then converted back to a tuple. Unwieldy idioms like this are likely to become more prevalent as more field-specific bugs are discovered - especially when those bugs are fixed in certain Satellite versions.

An empty tuple is used as the default argument to `read` because:

* tuples are immutable
* the field should default to an empty iterable

However, as described above, tuples are hard to work with. Some better solution should be conceived and implemented.

One alternative it to make `read` have this signature:

```python
def read(self, entity=None, attrs=None, ignore=None):
```

And somewhere in the call stack, `ignore` would be given a default value:

```python
if ignore is None:
    ignore = set()
```

This would work decently well in cases where an argument should always be ignored:

```python
def read(self, entity=None, attrs=None, ignore=None):
    if ignore is None:
        ignore = set()
    ignore.add('password')
```

It would also work very well in cases where more interesting manipulations need to happen:

```python
ignore.add([…])  # list comprehension
if 'errata_id' in attrs:
    ignore.discard('errata')  # pylint:disable=no-member
```

This solution would retain the two advantages listed earlier:

* <del>tuples are</del><ins>None is</ins> immutable
* the field should default to an empty iterable

issue_title: Drop extra attribute from OneTo*Field classes
issue_body:
Here, in full, the definition of the `OneToOneField` class:

```python
class OneToOneField(Field):
    """Field that represents a reference to another entity.

    All parameters not documented here are passed to :class:`Field`.

    :param entity: Either a class or the name of a class.
    :param module: A string. A dotted module name. Used by
        :meth:`OneToOneField.gen_value`. See that method for more details.

    """

    def __init__(self, entity, module=None, *args, **kwargs):
        self.entity = entity
        super(OneToOneField, self).__init__(*args, **kwargs)

    def gen_value(self):
        """Return the class that this field references."""
        return self.entity
```

What's wrong here? The class requires that a `module` argument be provided, and the argument is stored as an instance attribute. The docstring states that `module` should be "a dotted module name" and that it is used by the `gen_value` method. However, that's not true. In reality, the `module` attribute is absolutely useless. It does nothing. The `module` argument and attribute are outdated relics of when the `OneToOneField` was a class attribute. As a result, it should be removed.

Similar comments apply to `OneToManyField`.

# Issue Title

As I'm trying to create local mirrors for both ubntu 14.04 and 18.04 I'm encountering issues with how MS maintains its ubuntu mirrors

# General

According to [this page](https://www.aptly.info/doc/feature/duplicate/) which links to the debian [documentation on repository format](https://wiki.debian.org/DebianRepository/Format?action=show&redirect=RepositoryFormat) the following has to be true:
> A repository must not include different packages (different content) with the same package name, version, and architecture. When a repository is meant to be used as a supplement to another repository this should hold for the joint main+supplement repository as well.

As such, the current format of the 14.04 / 18.04 repositories:
http://packages.microsoft.com/ubuntu/14.04/prod
...
http://packages.microsoft.com/ubuntu/18.04/prod

contains, for every package the microsoft provides, multiple files with the same name but with different content, for example:
```
$:/tmp/truty/packages.microsoft.com/ubuntu$ sha1sum */aadlogin_1.0.005200001_amd64.deb
22d3fea107fe2c2fa9669532c051fc2728f2d5f0  14.04/aadlogin_1.0.005200001_amd64.deb
750137f3ba17423c5132440f0270a306d2297476  18.04/aadlogin_1.0.005200001_amd64.deb
```

This can be resolved rather easily by making sure the names of the package file names contain the name of the ubuntu distro, for example, take mono's packages:

```
mono-complete_5.12.0.226-0xamarin3+ubuntu1404b1_amd64.deb
mono-complete_5.12.0.226-0xamarin3+ubuntu1804b1_amd64.deb
```

which include the string `ubuntu1804` and `ubuntu1404` in their filenames...

issue_title: Renewer can give up when a single renewal fails
issue_body:
I'm not sure where this is happening in the renewer logic, but the renewer can give up and not attempt any subsequent lineage renewals if a single lineage renewal fails.

When fixing this we should also check that the renewer exits with nonzero status if any renewal failed (but that it still attempts the other ones).

- [ ] Docker container updated
- [ ] VirtualBox image updated
- [ ] How to Install, includes options

Pros and cons:

* Docker - Docker for Windows requires 64bit Windows 10 Pro with Hyper-V available. Nice parallel download of container.
* VirtualBox - download all at once. Entire Ubuntu.
* Colab - has GPU. Clunky. Saves on GDrive. Can collaborate.
* MyBinder. Subject to availability.

issue_title: Matlab doesn't notice files have changed when executing cells
issue_body:
I am using jupyter-hub and (prerelease) matlab r2015b, on ubuntu 14.04 and Mac OS X 10.10. My workflow is typically:

* Write matlab function in function.m
* Run function in notebook.ipynb using shift-return in a cell which contains the line function(foo, bar)
* Edit function.m
* Run function in notebook.ipynb using shift-return

When I do the last step, I find that the changes to function.m are not noticed by matlab, and it runs a (presumably cached) version of function.m. For example, if function.m contains a print statement, commenting this out has no effect when function.m is run from the notebook.

I have worked around this by adding an ls() to the first line of do_execute_direct, but obviously this is not the right fix:
```
    def do_execute_direct(self, code):
        self._matlab.run_code("ls;")
```

def inline_map(map):

issue_title: optionally project sequence alignment differences during mapping
issue_body:
Originally reported by: **Reece Hart (Bitbucket: [reece](http://bitbucket.org/reece), GitHub: [reece](http://github.com/reece))**

----------------------------------------

When mapping between two sequences with alignment discrepancies, the mapper currently projects only the variant itself. Optionally, mapping should also project sequence differences (e.g., reference-transcript discrepancies).

This is related to issue #168.

----------------------------------------
- Bitbucket: https://bitbucket.org/biocommons/hgvs/issue/169

This is what happened:

```
root@amaranta:/other/localbackup# /root/tools/backblaze/bin/b2 sync --skipNewer --delete ./ b2:amarantabackup
delete duplicity-inc.20160430T014501Z.to.20160501T014502Z.manifest.gpg (old version)

upload duplicity-inc.20160429T014502Z.to.20160430T014501Z.manifest.gpg
upload duplicity-inc.20160501T014502Z.to.20160502T014501Z.manifest.gpg

upload duplicity-new-signatures.20160501T014502Z.to.20160502T014501Z.sigtar.gpg

upload duplicity-inc.20160501T014502Z.to.20160502T014501Z.vol1.difftar.gpg
```

Unluckily, I hadn't saved a screenshot of the web gui before doing that, so I don't know what it did show.

But, rest assured again that the files were totally unmodified since they were originally uploaded, but the tool somehow detected they were changed (locale timestamp > remote timestamp?) and reuploaded them.

I'm including a full ```ls -l``` for the first reuploaded file:

```
root@amaranta:/other/localbackup# ls -l --time-style="full-iso" duplicity-inc.20160430T014501Z.to.20160501T014502Z.manifest.gpg 
-rw------- 1 root root 233 2016-05-01 03:49:12.653555465 +0200 duplicity-inc.20160430T014501Z.to.20160501T014502Z.manifest.gpg

```

I don't know whether the webgui is autodetecting my browser's timezone or doing geolocalization otherwise, or there's a "timezone stripping" issue which should be fixed.

Also, it seems that, by now, the "uploaded" field with the "sync" command doesn't actually save the "uploaded time", but the "original creation" time - which is a bit of a different beast, isn't it?

This behavior is unintended. Duplicate, valid entries should be considered valid.

issue_title: Add open() and close() to SerialBase
issue_body:
The `SerialBase` should have the `open()` and `close()` Method implemented with a `raise NotImplementedError()` like this:

````python
class SerialBase(io.RawIOBase):
    # All the existing code ...

    def open(self):
        """Opens the Port"""
        raise NotImplementedError("This function must be implemented in the subclass")

    def close(self):
        """Closes the Port"""
        raise NotImplementedError("This function must be implemented in the subclass")
````     

This way it is immediatly clear that this functions must be implemented in subclasses and if somenone is looking at the code of `SerialBase` its obvious where these methods are doing.

issue_title: Overriden parameter does not propagate to dictionary
issue_body:
I have a configuration specified as
```python
@test_ingredient.config
def ingredient_cfg():
    value = 200
    subconfig = {
        'value': value,
    ...
}
```
If I run my experiment as
```
python test.py with ingredient_config.value=42
```
The updated value is correctly propagated down to the `subconfig` dictionary, and I get:
```python
ingredient_config = {'value': 42, subconfig: {'value': 42}}
```

However, if I choose to override the height using another ConfigScope:
```python
@test_ingredient.config
def update_ingredient_cfg():
    value = 42
}
```

The value is only changed in the outer config, and not in the `subconfig` dictionary, and I end up with:
```python
ingredient_config = {'value': 42, subconfig: {'value': 200}}
```

Is this a bug or can I work around this somehow?
Thanks

issue_title: Common way to interact with python prompt
issue_body:
Building on #549 I think it would be sensible to plan some common framework for how we display some important messages to the user.
i.e. credential running out, flushing the in-memory logging, something of interest to user such as a large task reaching completion etc.

This could be done correctly in a Python/IPython agnostic way if we're clever I think but there is a call for presenting messages of this type to the user in a common way rather than pushing everything to the logging from another background thread.

This came up in discussion of this PR but is worth looking at again once that has been merged.

### Issue Description
I used librealsense for some months, and two months ago I updated once, there were no problems. And I saw many updates recently so I wanted to update again. After git pull and recompiling (followed the instruction cmake, make uninstall, make clean, make, make install...), I have a lot of troubles now. So far I can see, many examples do not work properly. Point cloud is strange displayed, live depth image is changing much slower than before, measure example crashes directly... 

I want to ask if this issue is familier, what should I do? Thank you!

| Required Info                         |                                                                |
|---------------------------------|------------------------------------------- |
| Camera Model                       | D410 | 
| Firmware Version                   | 05.08.14.00 | 
| Operating System & Version |   Win 10  | 
| Platform                                 | PC (C#.NET)  |
| SDK Version                            |  2.9.1 | 

Hi,

I have a device which has a D410 depth module with no RealSense colour camera, but it does also have a rear-facing webcam. I want to be able to map from the colour photo from the platform camera (webcam) to the depth data so I can tap on the colour image to get the corresponding depth value. The physical distance between the right lens of the depth camera and the colour camera is approx. (93mm, 17mm, 10mm), and they have different focal lengths, fields of view etc. I am using C# to further complicate the matter.

I've gotten the extrinsic calibration between the two cameras, as well as their individual intrinsics, using OpenCv; and I've gotten them both streaming at the same time (using `Sensor.Start` for the platform camera and `Pipeline.Start` for depth) but I'm struggling with doing the actual mapping.

I have tried several approaches: reverse engineering the `Align` class & implementing` align_images` myself in C# (wasn't mapping correctly & was extremely slow); exposing a new entry point into the existing `Align` class allowing me to inject my own extrinsics (`align.Process` always times out); and finally, using the new `SoftwareDevice` interface, which I've had a go at wrapping myself and seems fine up until I pass my `Syncer` object to `Sensor.Start` where I get a compile error that `Syncer` can't convert to `FrameQueue` or `FrameCallback`.

So anyway, my real question is: do any of these approaches sound reasonable? In your opinion, do you think what I want to do is possible with the current SDK (or with relatively small modifications to it)? 
It's entirely likely that I've made mistakes while modifying the SDK as I'm not very well versed in C++ or interop stuff, but I don't know if it's worth me spending the time going into it if it turns out that either I'll need to do loads of extra stuff beyond just getting the wrapper working, or you guys are working on something that'll make it really easy, or the differences between the cameras makes it a really hard problem to solve without lots of expertise (that I lack!)

Thanks for any help or advice you may be able to offer.
James

issue_title: Test the `repoview` option for the yum_distributor
issue_body:
`repoview` is an option which can be enabled in the config for the `yum_distributor`, it also enables `generate_sqlite` option, because sqlite files are used by the repoview tool.
If `repoview` is enabled, there will be an additional step responsible for the generation of the HTML files during publish.
As for the browsing, the HTTP request will be redirected to show a repoview content if it is available.

Suggestion for the test:
- create RPM repo
- sync and publish it
- observe no redirect and the directory list of packages when accesing repo content via HTTP(S).
- update `yum_distributor` with `repoview` option set to `True`
- re-publish repo
- observe redirect (302) to repoview content: https://path_to_the_repo/repoview/index.html

Redmine story: https://pulp.plan.io/issues/189

`pulp-admin docker repo uploads upload --repo-id=skopeo -f skopeo.tar`

`pulp-admin docker repo list`

issue_title: Graphql schema probably not suited for parallel execution
issue_body:
This is probably a minor issue as it occurs only on server startup.

I noticed that my django view (with heavy use of relay), when rendered right after django startup, tends to throw a variety of graphql errors.
It came out only with relay (that is - when multiple graphql queries were sent one by one).

After diving into the code I think I more or less analyzed the issue. `GraphQLView` reuses the same schema (`graphene.core.schema.Schema`) for execution. That schema  has `self._types` dictionary, which is constructed only once.
- When more than one thread interfere during initial `_types` construction, `Type {} already registered with other object type` errors are thrown inside `register` method.

That schema also creates `graphql.core.type.schema.GraphqlSchema`. I'm not sure how this is possible, as every time schema is accessed, a new instance is created, but here again looks like 
- thread interference leads to errors: `Schema must contain unique named types but contains multiple types named {}` in `type_map_reducer` function.

Below an example of an error. I don't paste all of them, but that is a good example, as many of them are related to automatically created Connection types, which, I guess, are created independently by separate threads traversing type tree and, so, are not the same types although they do have the same name (which I checked).

issue_title: "Decorated type deeper than introspection query" error with nested lists
issue_body:
We see the following errors when using nested lists:
```
Error: Decorated type deeper than introspection query.
    at n (http://cdn.jsdelivr.net/graphiql/0.7.1/graphiql.min.js:15:23637)
    at n (http://cdn.jsdelivr.net/graphiql/0.7.1/graphiql.min.js:15:23879)
    at n (http://cdn.jsdelivr.net/graphiql/0.7.1/graphiql.min.js:15:23733)
    at n (http://cdn.jsdelivr.net/graphiql/0.7.1/graphiql.min.js:15:23879)
    at r (http://cdn.jsdelivr.net/graphiql/0.7.1/graphiql.min.js:15:24425)
    at http://cdn.jsdelivr.net/graphiql/0.7.1/graphiql.min.js:15:26609
    at http://cdn.jsdelivr.net/graphiql/0.7.1/graphiql.min.js:11:8480
    at Array.reduce (native)
    at keyValMap (http://cdn.jsdelivr.net/graphiql/0.7.1/graphiql.min.js:11:8444)
    at f (http://cdn.jsdelivr.net/graphiql/0.7.1/graphiql.min.js:15:26484)
```
Here's the example code that can cause this issue. Graphene introspection doesn't report any errors. 
```
import graphene, json

class Animal(graphene.Interface):
   id = graphene.String()

class Dog(graphene.ObjectType):
   class Meta:
      interfaces = (Animal, )
   breed = graphene.String()

class Cat(graphene.ObjectType):
   class Meta:
      interfaces = (Animal, )
   category = graphene.String()

class Query(graphene.ObjectType):
  animals = graphene.NonNull(graphene.List(graphene.NonNull(graphene.List(graphene.NonNull(Animal)))))
  def resolve_animals(self, args, context, info):
    return[[Dog(id=1, breed='Golden Retriever'),
            Dog(id=2, breed='German Shepard')],
           [Cat(id=11, category ='Tiger'),
            Cat(id=12, category='House Cat')]]

schema = graphene.Schema(query=Query, types=[Dog, Cat, ])
```
Errors disappear if we remove the "required" or NonNull decorators. Any suggestions on how to fix these errors?

issue_title: Template source does not respect the INTERNAL_IPS setting
issue_body: The template_source view allows to be called from IP addresses outside of the INTERNAL_IPS list. This allows unpriviledged users to view the source code of templates and mabye also other files.

When clicking on the projector tab, tensorboard starts loading tensor values and computes the PCA.
The problem is that if the tensor is very large (e.g. large embeddings), default computing the PCA will take super long time, and you can do nothing but wait a few minutes at least.

I believe this is flaw for the default logic, a better option would be to let the user decide whether to compute the PCA(T-SNE, etc), for example, a button saying visualize.

 - TensorBoard version: 1.8.0
 - OS Platform and version: Ubuntu 16.04
 - Python version: 3.5

issue_title: Using a tuple for filter size causes crash
issue_body:
Using tflearn.conv_2d with a tuple as a filter size causes the crash:

> TypeError: type() takes 1 or 3 arguments

Seems to be caused by this section of utils.py, in autoformat_filter_conv2d:


    if isinstance(fsize,int):
        return [fsize, fsize, in_depth, out_depth]
    elif type(fsize, (tuple, list)):
        if len(fsize) == 2:
            return [fsize[0], fsize[1], in_depth, out_depth]
        else:
            raise Exception("filter length error: " + str(len(fsize))
                            + ", only a length of 2 is supported.")
    else:
        raise Exception("filter format error: " + str(type(fsize)))



The line:
` elif type(fsize, (tuple, list)):`
can be fixed to:
` elif isinstance(fsize, (tuple, list)):`

issue_title: 2D convolution on CIFAR10 example shows poor results
issue_body:
I posted this in stackoverflow, but it may be more appropriate here.
https://stackoverflow.com/questions/38885226/2d-convolution-in-tflearn-cnn-trashes-learning-for-mnist-and-cifar-10-benchmarks

Essentially I just want to make sure there isn't a bug in the 2D convolution or maxpooling functions. Following the tutorial script here:
https://github.com/tflearn/tflearn/blob/master/examples/images/convnet_cifar10.py

I get completely random classifications (10% validation accuracy), all the way through 50 epochs. Commenting out the 2d convolution and max pooling steps though, it goes up to about 50% validation accuracy. To me this sounds intuitively wrong (as if the convolution function is somehow accidentally shuffling the input data), but perhaps this example is simply a poor classifier.  Is this reproducible for others, or is there something wrong with my tflearn or tensorflow builds?

I noticed that the **Basic Usage Examples** page and the **Advanced Usage [Examples]** page both start out with some information about how to make a connection to some BigchainDB API endpoint server running somewhere.

I wonder if it would make sense to put that on a new separate page between **Quickstart / Installation** and **Basic Usage Examples**. Maybe it could be titled **Step 2: Connect**. It could have several sections, one for each case, starting with the easiest and most common. The **Basic Usage Examples** page and the **Advanced Usage [Examples]** page could start by saying they must be connected to a BigchainDB API endpoint server running somewhere, with a link to the new page if they need to do that.

issue_title: Allow private keys to be password protected
issue_body:
In some scenarios it would be desirable not to save unencrypted private keys on disk, for instance when they reside on untrusted locations (like VPSes without disk encryption support), and securing communications may be a concern.

For now I'm using a script than encrypts the saved key and deletes the original one, but I think a native solution would be preferable, also to avoid saving (even temporarily) plain keys on disk.

I also understand that automation is one of the main goals of LE, but considering than many servers allow for the key's password to be entered on startup (Apache is for instance one of them), such an option may be implemented as an optional switch that have to be explicitly activated when needed.

As suggested [here](https://github.com/certbot/certbot/issues/3782#issuecomment-282555091), we should make a certbot-large image for people who want additional utilities available for use with hooks. Thoughts:

* It should build off our current Dockerfile
* We should stick to common tools we expect people will need and use, but should err on the side of including extra stuff size given the name of the image. At the very least, we need `curl` (see #4552).

cc @joohoi

issue_title: Certbot crashes if EFF e-mail submission endpoint is unavailable
issue_body:
Certbot will crash if the user's e-mail information can't be submitted to the EFF endpoint:

https://community.letsencrypt.org/t/issue-when-trying-to-create-certificate/59307/1

https://github.com/certbot/certbot/blob/master/certbot/eff.py#L74

It crashes with a somewhat relevant error if the endpoint is unavailable, but a confusing error if the endpoint returns something other than JSON. It should probably not crash at all in either case. If the endpoint specifically says that the user's e-mail address is malformed, that's probably worth stopping to report to the user, but if the endpoint simply doesn't reply usefully, it's probably preferable to continue since I wouldn't think we really want to make users wait in this situation.

(Adding the "bug" tag at least for the case where the endpoint returns a non-JSON reply because the error shown to the user in this case is unhelpful.)

![screen shot 2017-10-12 at 4 17 48 pm](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/181744/31519737-f6103838-af68-11e7-8c0a-66c22b1233b1.png)

```python
import ipywidgets
from IPython.display import display

out1 = ipywidgets.Output()
tab = ipywidgets.Tab([out1], layout=ipywidgets.Layout(height='400px'))

    for _ in range(40):
        print("foo")

tab.set_title(0, "bar")
tab
```

* Python 3.6.2
* ipywidgets 7.0.1
* notebook 5.1.0
* Chrome 61
* Mac OS 10.12.6

issue_title: Dataset add `--match-rules` option, is it still needed?
issue_body:
It's not super clear how to use it and who is using it. It is not being tested by our integration tests.

https://coveralls.io/builds/16986888/source?filename=datacube%2Fscripts%2Fdataset.py#L91

Reading code it seems to be something like:
 
> Rather than using product definition from the database load them from file

It's unclear exactly how different definitions in the "rules files" can  be from what's in database. I do not see reasonable use cases for this option. I propose we remove it.

issue_title: Discussion: should trio.ssl be imported by default?
issue_labels: [{'id': 523592585, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/python-trio/trio/labels/design%20discussion', 'name': 'design discussion', 'color': '0052cc', 'default': False}, {'id': 523593177, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/python-trio/trio/labels/polish', 'name': 'polish', 'color': 'f9d0c4', 'default': False}, {'id': 538689361, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/python-trio/trio/labels/potential%20API%20breaker', 'name': 'potential API breaker', 'color': 'b60205', 'default': False}]
issue_body:
Currently it is. Glyph says that's bad, because there are cases where you have lots of tiny processes stuffed onto a box and openssl is kinda memory hungry, so it's better not to load the `ssl` module until you have to.

I'm not super-opposed to making people `import trio.ssl`, but if we have a high-level `trio.open_ssl_tcp_stream(...)` function then it may take some awkward work to avoid importing it early. And I'm not a big fan of the idea that calling that function would implicitly cause `trio.ssl` to materialize in the user-visible namespace.

### Issue Description

[INSERT DESCRIPTION OF THE PROBLEM]

Our **README.md** is too large, we need to reorganise the content. Move specific topic to dedicated files, and only keep links on **README.md**

When trying to run a 96-core machine, I get the following error:
```bash
["Error in 2--james--[redacted] (task: 2) - code 3: Execution failed: creating instance: inserting instance: Instances using machine types with more vCPUs than 64 or more memory than 455GB must have their minimum CPU platform set to 'skylake' or higher."]
```

In the console, I normally use `--min-cpu-platform "Intel Skylake"` to enable the use of 96-core machines, but this is not an option with dsub. Is this something that might be added in the future?

┆Issue is synchronized with this [JIRA Story](https://ucsc-cgl.atlassian.net/browse/TOIL-299)
┆Issue Number: TOIL-299

We need to do a pass on naming definitions. At minimum inputs and outputs should consistently by input_defs and output_defs. This is mostly because python is dumb and has "input" as a keyword which no one ever uses. So you are either stuff with `input_` or `input_def` or similar. Probably all of these things (solids, arguments, contexts) should be suffixed with def by default but I do still react negatively to that.
